change.item: correct spelling of *Mandra[sena] and *Saṃghabhara included, more corrections proposed by Funayama Toru included
"Related Persons", "Date", "Research History" by Zhang Zong added

figure.head: 鐵山鳥瞰圖，熱氣球低空拍攝；2005年拍攝
Aerial photograph of the inscription site on Mount Tie, taken in 2005

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 鐵山位於鄒城北端，山體東西長0.9公里，南北寬0.4公里。峰頂海拔為148米，山腳處海拔110米。其東北一側1.5公里處是崗山。就地理坐標而言，鐵山位於WGS84坐標系的東經116.961度，北緯35.415度，相當於在UTM坐標系統第50S帶東行496470，北行3919020。

目前，鐵山南坡已改建為公園，內有幾個亭子和一間門衛室，都為現代建築物。數排住宅樓一直延伸到山體南面約200米處（地圖，；）。山體東面有最大的一片岩坡，其三角形的石面平坦裸露，朝向南面，底部長約57米，向上延伸約92米，斜面傾斜度大體一致，為45%至50%度。石面頂部灌木叢生，與鐵山山巔相距僅30米。山體的主坡沿中軸線略為鼓起，其較低矮的西側也覆有植被。石面如此便十分適合刻鑿大篇文字，而石經也恰恰刊刻於此（）。刻經朝向東南方向4公里之外的陽山，亦與相隔11公里宏偉的嶧山遙遙相望。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 鐵山刻銘呈巨碑形狀，總高度約為51.70米，斜臥在山坡上，採用了當時立碑的通行形式。右下角刻有巨龜為基座，其背上六邊形紋樣清晰可辨（）。此龜佔據基座寬度僅一半，因而推測該虛擬巨碑的刻銘底下原作雙龜碑座。
雙龜碑座形式頗為罕見。是朝鮮新羅朝9或10世紀的一個實例。《石頌》（鐵山2號）一處也提到“雙龜”。但左邊的一隻毫無痕跡。右邊一隻尚可辨認，其姿勢引人注目，面朝前方，頭部高昂，眼、鼻和喙都清晰可辨（）。洪頂山北坡則刻有單隻贔屭，上承刊刻《摩訶衍經》的石碑，《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第125－130頁。但保存狀況遠遜鐵山。

碑體為34.7×13.8米，刻有《大方等大集經》《大方等大集經》T#397, 13: 1－407。節文，分為兩大塊面，每塊八行。兩個塊面之間，留出寬約一米的空行，較文字行略寬。這條過道可供修行者上下走動，誦讀毗鄰經文時不致踩踏字面。一張攝於20世紀40年代的照片證明，當時的植被並不繁茂，這條過道似乎指向山脊處一塊突出的雙叉形岩石。道端良秀第15頁。刻經每行57至62字不等，末行第17行最短，僅有六個字。此行不同於其他行，並不從頂端開始，其第一個字與第16行第9字“名”字高度相同。此行下方兩個孤立的小字“安”（位於第16行第18字“訶”的左邊）和“上”，不是經文的一部分。通過復原可知，刻經原本有946字。排印本 《大正藏》所收這段節文僅930字。鐵山所刻脫兩字，衍八字。某些行起首重複上一行末尾一到四個字，因此多出10個字。

鐵山依照其三維原型，碑形刻經面上方亦有碑額，13.2×18 米見方（）。碑體的過道徑直通向縱向的長方形碑額，上題“大雧經”三個大字。碑額8.51×3.31米，圍以約30釐米寬的雙線邊框，邊框數處由筆畫側鋒疊壓，碑額三字也為水平狀雙線隔開。繁複的“雧”字位於不規則石面略微凸起的地方，這表明經文是斟酌之後上石的。經題為殘存的翻捲線條所環繞，線條是交纏的龍身的一部分。將這些線條與立碑的裝飾相比較，看出可能龍頭是朝向外面，龍嘴幾乎觸及碑身的上緣。它們踡起的背部可能比經題的上端横線高出4米。
20世紀80年代，為保存刻銘，四周圍牆上方又加立起護欄，牆內鑿刻階梯。碑額還有一條窄道橫貫上述“雧”字的底部以及左右兩邊的龍紋。那道護欄沒有顧及刻經原狀，與縱行經文不盡一致。其頂端為三角形，並未採用常見的拱形（）。儘管如此，站在鐵山這面斜坡之下，觀者無不為刻銘的巨大體量而動容（）。
碑狀刻經區左下部的外側刊刻大段銘文，題為《石頌》（鐵山2號），凡十二行，約17米長、4米寬。《石頌》部分的右上角與經文部分第16行第34字“法”銜接，刻字區略向左偏，因而右下角距相鄰經文約85釐米（）。字號小於碑狀經文，但標題的兩字則不然，“石”字為85×76釐米，“頌”字72×77釐米，採用莊重的篆書，高踞《石頌》全文之上。

《大集經》節文末排左下角約16米，但仍在護欄之內，平整的岩面上有一條題記，列出了三個人物的名字及官銜──閭長嵩、僧安道壹（活動於562－579年）和孫洽（鐵山3號；和）。中間一行為僧人名字，其獨特之處在於兩邊各空一行，並且如同上方經題一樣置於雙欄格內。在鐵山這種格子僅此兩例。另外，鐵山曾存有一則題記，提及人名“李巨敖”。儘管題記已不復存在，卻可以根據鄒城博物館所藏拓片復原這則題記的完整內容（鐵山4號；）。該題記原在圍牆壁外面的東邊，但為採石場毀於1960年。 胡新立第250頁。賴非第123頁。

div.head: 釋名
div.p: 山體平矮如崗，初名南崗山、前崗山。《石頌》記此名：“瑕丘東南大崗山南崗之陽……”。 下文鐵山2號部分，第頁。 婁一均於清康熙五十四年（1715）所修《鄒縣志》載：
張丕矩修民國《山東鄒縣地理志》：“前崗山刻《大集經》。”《鄒縣舊志匯編》 第25頁。

由吳若灝於光緒年間（1875－1908）修訂的《鄒縣續志》（光緒十八年［1892］作序）稱此山為鐵山，“在城北四里，崗山之前”。《鄒縣續志》第521頁。 據當地居民說，《大集經》刻石面西側石窩為鐵拐李腳印。相傳其負經過此，遇雨，只好在此石坪上曬經。經文即是曬經留下的痕跡。有關泰山經石峪《金剛經》的傳說故事與此如出一轍。這處刻經附近的明代文人刻銘題記稱這個地方為“暴經石”或“曬經石”，也許暗指吳承恩（約1500－約1582年）著名小說《西遊記》中的一個故事。玄奘（唐三藏）師徒自西天返回途中落入“通天河”，他們隨後“卻移經於高崖上，開包曬晾。至今彼處曬經之石尚存。”見《西遊記》第880頁。 由此謂此山為鐵拐李山，簡稱“鐵山”。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 《鄒縣志》載，鄒縣城區北門有法興寺。遺址今在市區內，佔地約兩千平方米。北朝後期法興寺由嶧山遷移至此，並由縣衙重建，元代重修，更名重興寺。寺內有宋代磚塔。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 鐵山刻經涉及的人物見於《石頌》和另外兩則歷史題記。
一般論述鐵山石刻的學者，無不倍加關注《石頌》。《石頌》不僅讚頌了《大集經》書法的傑出成就，而且其本身書法也相當出色。另外，頌文敘述刻經原委，讚美漢代丞相匡衡的後代。學界普遍認為，匡氏家族率眾在此摩崖刻石。阮元《山左金石志》稱《石頌》作“匡喆刻經頌”，這一叫法沿用至今。
經文、《石頌》和題記之間的複雜關係，反映了政府官員、強宗大族和僧人書家之間的互動。其實，尖山也有類似的協作。
在每個石刻活動中，佛經供養人即“經主”是最重要的人物，負責籌措和支配資金。大沙門僧安道壹在鐵山和尖山分別與當地的大家族匡氏和韋氏合作，從事刻經活動。然而這兩個刻經活動的經主的社會地位很不相同。

figure.head: 鐵山刻銘分佈空拍正視圖；鐵山4號為推斷位置
Rectified aerial view of the inscription site on Mount Tie, showing the positions of Tie 1–4. The position of Tie 4 is hypothetical

div.p: 鐵山刻經晚尖山刻經四年，但此數年間經歷了滅佛運動和改朝換代。573年，北周武帝（561－578年在位）開始迫害佛教徒，577年滅北齊之後，迫害活動蔓延至今山東地區。578年，武帝薨歿，滅佛運動隨之告終。尖山三則刻銘（尖山2號、尖山4號和尖山7號）紀年為575年。顯然，刻經活動在滅佛運動波及山東之前就已經開始。與之相反，北周大像元年（579）鐵山石刻作於滅佛運動終止之後。它們較之尖山刻銘的規模要大得多，儼然作於滅佛之後佛教凱旋的時期，預示著581年繼位的隋文帝治下佛教的復興。

有人認為，《石頌》“乃率邑人”句指的是匡喆等領導同村人實施了刻經活動。但匡氏家族其實既非經主，也非官員。頌文還寫到“乃與同義人”和“歸財法肆”、“仍（乃）割家貲”。“同義人”指的是北朝盛行的民間社團邑義或義邑，這些社團的成立旨在追求佛教的功德事業，即刊刻佛經或刻鑿佛像。實際上由匡氏兄弟和“李桃、湯□、𡛏”委託製作石經。這些施主為此共同出資。由此可知，漢丞相匡衡後裔匡氏兄弟率村邑眾人刻經。此“邑”是指佛教社團邑義，邑中人共同出資，以求功德和禮佛。匡氏兄弟雖自視為邑中之首，卻無邑義組織者的正式身份，也未被明確稱作經主。經文題記（鐵山3號和鐵山4號）提供了更多資料。
這些題記提到，書丹人大沙門僧安道壹，以及經主，地方官任城郡守加銜寧朔將軍和大都督孫洽，還有“分侍左右”的兩位大都維那，即北齊任城郡功曹、北周平陽縣功曹趙郡李巨敖和北齊搜陽好人、平越將軍、北周任城郡主簿閭長嵩。
上文所及“都維那”並非職官名稱，而是源自邑義中僧官這一頭銜。李巨敖和閭長嵩都有官銜，既非僧人，也非僧官。“都維那”在此處指邑義組織的主事者。北朝邑義造像和造像碑的題記，多有“維那”、“都維那”之名，僅指主事佛教功德活動的人。“維那”一詞見於眾多造像題記，意為造像活動的參與者。因此，鐵山的兩位“大都維那”是指這項刻經活動的組織者、主事人，而不是僧團官員。
鐵山刻經涉及官員、望族和僧人，其中的匡氏家族並非當地官員，主要是作為捐資人統領信眾。由上可知，鐵山刻經由匡氏兄弟、李桃和其他邑義中人出資；地方官員擔當經主；寧朔將軍孫洽及其屬下李巨敖和閭長嵩負責組織，並行使都維那職事。《石頌》和其他題記從未提及刻工，但我們不應忘記，僧安道壹所書《大集經》，正是憑藉刻工辛苦勞作才付諸刻石。

討論相關人物，還應當提到《石頌》中僧安道壹的稱號“大沙門安法師”，這表明此僧可能姓安。李佐賢認為：“所謂安法師者，即安道一無疑也。”《石泉書屋金石題跋》第14195頁。在他之前，金石學家段松苓持論相近：“安道一者，似安姓，道一名也。”另外，段松苓評述尖山銘刻（尖山1號）時，逕稱“安道一”，而銘刻作“沙門僧安道壹”字。《山左碑目》第14844頁。然而，金石學家不如當今學者，能認識山東省所有刻經。事實上，“安道壹”這一稱謂沒有出現在任何一處刻經中，“僧安道壹”則是出現次數最多的一種名諱。鐵山3號題記中，該僧稱作“東嶺僧安道壹”。近來，羅炤第315頁，註1，有力地支持了“僧安道壹”這一名稱形式。亦見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第58、116、143頁，“討論”部分。 但是，應該注意的是，“安”為粟特姓氏，許多冠以該名者為傑出的佛教徒或譯經者。見福安敦 以及魏義天第77－79頁。

figure.head: 鐵山南麓及碑形刻經址全景；2007年拍攝
Comprehensive view of Mount Tie’s southern slope with the inscription site in the shape of a stele; photograph taken in 2007

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 鐵山摩崖包括經文、《石頌》和題記三部分，尚無法確定彼此之間的關係。王思禮和賴非第25－27頁探討了斷代問題。經文為主體，面積達千餘平方米，《石頌》佔地約為經文的四分之一，題記更小，是《石頌》尺寸的四分之一左右。

《石頌》和題記是先後刻鑿，交替進行還是同時完成，關於這一問題近些年有了一些進展。鐵山摩崖刻經的航拍照片攝製於2004年（），來自中德合作山東北朝摩崖刻經調查與研究項目。鳥瞰圖顯示在鐵山刻經的中央空出一行，將經文分成相等的兩部分（），《石頌》在其西面。考慮到兩個文本的空間配置，有可能它們是在開始刊刻之前同時規劃的。這對於“尋師珤翰”詞句是一個不同的新理解。一些學者以前將此句闡釋為佛經刊刻完之後再尋訪書家，因而頌文較經文為晚。

《石頌》記述了一個確鑿的紀年：皇周大像元年，歳大淵獻，八月庚申朔，十七日丙子（公元579年9月23日）。這種紀年方式在刻經題跋尾端部分極為罕見。《石頌》明言鐵山經文由僧安道壹書丹，即“《大集經·穿菩提品》九百卅字”，鄉賢匡氏等助資，“約石圖碑”，刻字上石，並分別在摩崖上方和下方刻出龍形和龜形。《石頌》韻文和散文兩部分都一再褒揚捐資助經的匡氏兄弟、李桃等功德無量，《石頌》還特別提到僧安道壹書藝超群，措辭不同尋常。《石頌》作者暗示，他撰寫頌文與鐵山刻經上石幾乎同時。書丹者雖為一人，但施主卻有多位，刻工必然不只一人，所以，鐵山摩崖刻經所有部分極有可能同時完工於579年。
自18世紀晚期以降，鐵山刻經進入金石學研究的視野。學者們對刻經的擘窠大字和《石頌》的生動氣韻欣賞有加。他們的著錄包括簡要目錄、全文轉錄和品評。因此，如本卷導論所言，鐵山可以作為研究中國金石學發展的個案。

早期的金石學家專注於個別的字，研究工作往往依賴拓片，幾乎不會躬身前往，踏訪刻經址。由此導致他們的研究不系統、不完整。不過黃易（黃小松，1744－1802）身先示範。他不僅勘訪原址，而且作了一幅鐵山圖（）。這是他1797年完成的二十四開冊葉《岱麓訪碑圖》之一。《蓬萊宿約》第190頁。韓文彬第159頁，圖3.3。普林斯頓大學藝術博物館藏一件類似冊葉也有一開表現鐵山，見劉怡瑋等第474頁，第5葉。有關黃易的冊葉，參見本卷“導論”，第頁。他在題記中描述了刻經址和刻銘，並提到在陡滑的石崖上打製拓片的情形。他對鐵山刻經的書法讚不絕口：“文辭雄麗，與岱麓磨崖刻爭勝。”他雖未能辨識經本，但確認經主為匡喆。
1796年夏，阮元（1764－1849）收到黄易寄呈的《石頌》（鐵山2號）的拓片，由此得知匡喆。阮元的《山左金石志》採用了這些拓片，次年此書付梓。卷十“北周石”載有兩則小鐵山刻石條目。第一則題為“匡喆刻經頌”，即《石頌》部分錄文。第二則為“小鐵山摩崖殘字八種”，即鐵山3號和鐵山4號題記，以及鐵山1號刻經節文的四個片段。《山左金石志》第14484－14486頁。
孫星衍（1753－1818）於嘉慶七年（1803）刊刻《寰宇訪碑錄》，卷二有五條著錄與鐵山相關，前三條斷定為“小鐵山”刻銘，即關於《石頌》（鐵山2號）的兩條，和題為“小鐵山摩崖殘字八種”的一條，此條出自阮元的著作。其他兩條內有趙郡李巨敖題記（鐵山4號）和寧朔將軍孫洽（鐵山3號）題記。《寰宇訪碑錄》第19873頁。
劉聲木（1876－1959）著《寰宇訪碑錄校勘記》有三條與鐵山有關：第一條是“小銕山匡喆刻經頌”，指出紀年“大象元年八月”；第二條是“小銕山摩崖殘字八種”，劉聲木認為此即“佛經題名八種”；第三條是“寧朔將軍孫洽等題名”（鐵山3號），劉氏注明此條內容已見“小銕山摩崖殘字八種”。《寰宇訪碑錄校勘記》第20117頁。
李佐賢（1807－1876）是少數實地勘查鐵山刻經的金石學家之一。其《石泉書屋金石題跋》記錄他於1839年踏訪刻經址，著錄銘刻行格，並確認《石頌》中的“安法師”與鐵山3號題記提到的為同一人。他將刻經的書風比照泰山經石峪。另外，他每每以己之見對照阮元之說，以期訂正阮元的錯誤。李佐賢見過今已不存的李巨敖題記三行，事實上該題記凡四行。刻於經文東側。《石泉書屋金石題跋》第14195頁。
吳式芬（1796－1856）所撰《金石彙目分編》著錄了所有鐵山刻銘，包括《大集經》、《石頌》，以及寧朔將軍孫洽、東嶺僧安道壹、閭長嵩和李巨敖的題記。吳式芬還著錄“安上”二字，並單獨作為一個條目，因而鐵山摩崖刻銘的總數合計七種。《金石彙目分編》第21153頁。
汪鋆（1816－1884－）的《十二硯齋金石過眼錄》中所錄鐵山銘刻多於阮元的版本，並稱經眼阮元手書拓本題簽。他也依從孫星衍《寰宇訪碑錄》，但混淆了尖山、鐵山和崗山的銘刻：鐵山3號左半部的兩條題記見於尖山之下，而右半部的題記在崗山之下。經文（鐵山1號）中的二十個字置於小鐵山之下，但在此也能見到崗山銘刻。《十二硯齋金石過眼錄》第7851－7852頁；第7856－7857頁。
《鄒縣金石續志》由錢枱編纂，刊行於1892年，《鄒縣金石續志》第136頁。 引錄了董純和馬星翼於1834年編寫的《鄒縣志稿》。《鄒縣志稿》有關鐵山的條目“北周大象元年刻石”，介紹了刻經的行格、斷代和經文的起訖句，以及《石頌》的行格、紀年和頌詞，還有石崖東邊和南邊題記的行數為十行。《鄒縣志稿》第469頁a。
孫葆田（1840－1911）在《山東金石志》中詳盡著錄了《石頌》和“北齊寧朔將軍孫洽題名”、“北齊東嶺僧題名”、“北齊搜揚好人題名”和“北周匡喆刻經頌”諸條題記。但是，未著錄篇幅最大的《大集經》。《山東金石志》第9245－9246頁；第9253－9254頁。 康有為於1890年，葉昌熾於1901年，楊守敬於1911年均盛讚鐵山石刻，但只是泛泛而談。參見本卷“導論”，第頁。
楊守敬（1839－1915）在其《學書邇言》一書中稱道《石頌》（鐵山2號）的結體和用筆優雅。他指出，包世臣（1775－1855）“未見小鐵山、岡山、尖山諸全刻，但見《文殊般若》一碑”，就將它“定為西晉人之作，誤也”。楊守敬第22－23頁。見上文。
20世紀40年代，道端良秀目睹此山刻字保存良好，並視之為中國佛教摩崖石刻的佼佼者。道端良秀第13－19頁。

div.head: 銘文總目
div.p: 鐵山刻有一段長篇佛經節文（鐵山1號）和三則歷史題記（鐵山2號、鐵山3號和鐵山4號；）。鐵山4號僅存拓本。

鐵山巨碑型刻經區的佛經選自曇無讖（385－433）譯《大方等大集經》。有關譯者曇無讖的研究，見鐮田茂雄第80－82頁。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷洪頂山2號，第103－107頁。通常認為該譯本完成於420年。通行的本子由十二品構成，凡六十卷。這段刻經節文見該經第五品《海慧菩薩品》，即卷八、九、十、十一。《大方等大集經》T#397, 13: 46b－74c。鐵山刻經節文對應於《大方等大集經》T#397, 13: 50a16－c13。韓文彬將《海慧菩薩品》誤作《大集經》第八品（第2頁）。如正文所言，此品應作該經第五品；我們討論的刻經節文恰好見現存《大集經》卷八。參見“討論”部分的王鈞條目（第頁）和相川政行條目（第頁）。

《大集經》在其流傳過程中似乎並非一成不變。據《出三藏記集》，僧祐（445－518）昔見的《大集經》版本為二十九卷十二品，第五品是《海慧菩薩品》，這與傳世版本一樣。《出三藏記集》T#2145, 55: 63a23－28。但智昇（活動於728－740年）《開元釋教錄》明確記錄，“今檢經本與祐記不同”，在他所見的本子中《海慧菩薩品》為《大集經》第四品。《開元釋教錄》T#2154, 55: 588a16－17。如前所述，僧祐見到的傳世版本並非如此。鐵山《石頌》聲稱刻於該地的經文段落取自《穿菩提心品》，而非《海慧菩薩品》。這恰恰體現了韓文彬所說經本的“流動性”（fluidity）， 韓文彬第12頁，註6。韓文彬第162－163頁；第328頁，註13。他準確無誤地指出，《穿菩提心品》這一品名並不存在，至少未見於傳世的版本。但是，智昇《開元釋教錄》列出了《穿菩提心經》一名，凡三紙（大抵相當於今《大正藏》的一頁），據稱抄自一個與《大寶積經》相關的經文，名為《海慧菩薩品》，但今本失收該品。《開元釋教錄》T#2154, 55: 651c25。有關經文教義的討論，見石井公成第65－78頁討論《大集經》的章節。《大集經》較早的本子，見敦煌寫本S.6388和S.613V。

《大集經》中的另兩段刻於他處。《六波羅蜜》節文《六波羅蜜》T#397, 13: 50b15－18。見於洪頂山2號刻銘；《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第103－107頁。 其後節文《論四無量心》《論四無量心》T#397, 13: 50b18－20。刻於南響堂山2號窟入口處壁面。參見蔣人和第239頁。刻鑿於南響堂山2號窟入口處壁面上的節文糅合了廣為流傳的《文殊師利所說般若波羅蜜經》九十八字節文（T#232, 8: 731a15－21）和《大集經》中的《論四無量心》節文。這一刻銘的拓片出版於《北京圖書館藏中國歷代石刻拓本彙編》第8冊：第46頁。

鐵山刻經選用的經本依照大乘菩薩觀全面描述證悟菩提次第。佛陀提問：“云何名為穿菩提心？”佛陀為之作答，展示覺悟自始至終的道路。凡人與菩薩的區別在於心（citta），菩薩發心要證大乘菩提，即無上正等正覺。經文結尾聲稱佛陀的這個教諭導引無量眾生得菩薩道。他們為了在娑婆世界中陷於生死輪迴的眾生而精誠修行。他們的修行集中於理解宇宙這個諸法世界的本性，並參透其本義。一旦認識本真，他們就能轉變娑婆世界，與佛同列，並最後將眾生渡至彼岸。供養人發起刻鑿鐵山刻經節文的願望和動機或許正在於此。見下文鐵山2號部分，第頁。

就形式和內容而言，鐵山刻經分為兩個主要部分。第一部分闡釋法界（dharmadhātu）的本性。《普林斯頓佛教辭典》第244頁，對法界的闡釋如下：“在大乘佛教中，‘法界’首先是被用來表示‘法的界域’，它指的是一切法的活動所發生於其中的無窮領域，即宇宙。它也是用於表示終極真實的少數術語中的一個……佛教修行的目的是認識並參與這個真實的境界。” 它包含一系列次第分明的普遍佛教教義，它們為菩薩所“知”或“了知”，還包括一系列“觀”，菩薩由此達到更深層次的認識，進而臻於更深級的觀想。經文清晰呈示不同的菩薩行，這一部分主要以駢文寫成。刻經第二部分詳盡闡述佛法（buddha­dharma）的要義。《普林斯頓佛教辭典》（第151頁）如此論及佛法（buddhadharma）：“梵文中意為‘佛的教諭’，是與英文所稱的‘Buddhism’最接近的印度的對應詞。這個詞也被用於指‘獨有的因素’，即佛所特有的一系列十八種（有時多至140種）特殊性質（dharma 法），例如正念、不會犯身體和言語上的錯誤，或一切構成佛的性質。”Gethin（第238－239頁）不願將巴利文的“dhamma”（或梵文dharma）譯作“教諭”，他寫道：“在存在佛教傳統問題的語境中，很容易將‘dharma’譯作‘教諭’（teaching）或‘教義’（doctrine），但這未能傳達出‘法’（dhamma）觀念的一個基本的微妙內涵。在求助於七種菩提分法（Seven Sets）時，‘法’的觀念中內涵有一種模棱兩可，一如同時指真理本身（指可以被直接了解和實現的真理）和關於真理的教諭（指示真理如何被直接了解和實現的教導）。”它的結構沒有那麼謹嚴，文風為一種勸誡，佛在其中又五次稱修行者為“善男子”。這種稱謂適合於佛教的在家弟子，因而適合於鐵山上經文的一切讀者。
鐵山1號中英文對照和英譯本（見下文第頁）的版式與經文的結構相應。刻經內容可以概述如下。

在引言中，佛陀表明他將闡釋如何能夠認識菩提心。凡人之心起“像似我慢”，因為凡人把自己視為獨特一員，不會對之起疑。這樣的凡人，要想成為佛弟子，必須首先“發菩提心”，以成菩薩。然而，佛陀發出了第一個警告：菩提心非可著、可貪、可愛之物，甚至不可觀想，因為執著、貪求、愛慕、觀想，乃至錯誤的觀想，都是引發凡人之心而非菩薩心的行為。當止息所有的執著、貪求、愛慕和觀想，菩薩就能使“心寂靜”。惟有心寂，方能確保正確的觀想參透“深法界”和“諸佛法”。
引言末尾出現的法界和佛法這兩個概念可以視為經文主體的兩大段落（I和II）的標題。第一段落對法界的解釋可細分為三個主題：一、認識法界（I.1）；二、參與法界（I.2）；三、轉化法界（I.3）。通過觀察諸法的本性，菩薩首先需要理解世界如何真實構成，然後能夠參與其中並完成其種種修行，最終將世界轉化為具有終極真實的法界。
第一個主題（I.1）與認識法界有關，始於佛教世界觀的基礎，即十二因緣教義。宇宙諸法相互依存，因為因緣而持續不斷地產生和滅亡。沒有什麼持久不變，沒有什麼停留不動，哪怕只是一瞬間。永久或存在的概念無非只是幻象，一切皆無自我，亦無可以認定為支撐“自性”的核心。通過對緣起的觀想，菩薩洞察一切現象核心的空性，並且了解到既無自我，也無主體，亦無造作者。
在這一點上，修行者或許處於陷入虛無主義的險地，而菩薩則信奉三種三昧。三種三昧認為，空性是正確理解世界本來面目的關鍵。
菩薩在理解了諸法相緣而生的本性之後，隨即求助於五蘊。五蘊構成了萬物聚合而成的本性，同時，菩薩認識到這些同樣為空。浮沫、水泡和海市蜃樓，連同香蕉樹的空心樹幹和迷惑人的幻象，是對非持久性和無實在性的譬喻。
他的下一個觀想使菩薩認識到物質世界的現象既無造作也不動搖，而與之相類，萬物不生，因為它們的“自我”並無著落，不像其迷惑性的感官和永無寧息的意識使其相信的那般。在此意義上，宇宙的一切法，包括外在世界無造作的現象，以及所有無生的諸生，彼此並不相異。它們是“一味、一乘、一道、一原”。
菩薩隨後觀想世界的聲音，覺其既無實質也不連貫，並且認識到由聲音構成的言語並不足以描述諸法的真實本質。當此處的經文以“聲”或“音”為觀想對象時，可能暗示《法華經》中眾所周知的觀世音菩薩，他聆聽並回應著世上受苦眾生的聲音。的確，經本接下來是菩薩對四諦的充分理解，即“苦”，“集”，“滅”，“道”。
如今菩薩“知道畢竟”，於是在第二個主題（I.2）能夠通過修行參與法界，所依據者是在所有佛教傳統中被稱為“三十七道品”的名相。 第14頁。這三十七個要素由七組特殊的修行構成， 第280頁進一步總結：“七種菩提分法早在巴利文經典的四原始尼柯耶時期，就已牢固地確立為一個獨特而分立的序列”。 即：

list.item: 四念處；
四正勤；
四神足（或稱四如意足）；
五根；
五力；
七覺支；
八正道。

div.p: 為了從阻礙自己領悟真實的認知障中解放出來，菩薩依照一組名為四念處的修行，細查自身的心靈與感受。在體認到身體、感受、心靈和外在世界所有現象的空性後，菩薩獲得了法界的知識。

後兩組修行未逐字見於鐵山刻經。四正勤概括為正勤，四神足則為如意足。之後的四組修行則完整列出：五根、五力、七覺支和八正道。這四組中的每一個因素不僅列出名字，而且還以一個短語加以解釋，共計25行駢文。每一行由八個字組成，其中四個字節是一個要素的涵義，另四個字則給出它的名稱。在論及八正道時，還額外加了兩個四字短語。一個為了解釋“正業”、“正命”、“正精進”關乎“身、口、意”，一個則是解釋“正念”和“正定”涉及倫理層面，關係到“善不善”的問題。通過這些四字短語的解釋，《大集經》“穿菩提心品”以獨特的大乘方式闡述了久為人知的三十七道品。三十七道品是以菩薩之心加以實踐，因此成為將法界轉化為究竟真實的基礎。

論法界的第三個主題（I.3）聲稱實相的本性寂靜。在其根本意義上，一切存在的三個特性“無常、苦、無我”都是清淨的。此外，習慣上被稱為清淨者其實是究竟真實的大淨。與其他傳統中為人所知的三十七道品相異，如今菩薩通過完成特定的大乘菩薩行，即六波羅蜜和四無量心，以參與法界。就此而言，在此所呈現的《大集經》節文可以被描述成許多大乘經典中所見的三十七道品的擴展目錄。第275頁引用了梵文版《大般若經》中的一個例子。由此，他領悟到一切法無一例外皆是：

list.item: 未來世清淨；
過去有種種相；
現在無我。

div.p: 菩薩因而不僅掌握了過去、現在和未來，他也理解了究竟真實──如今它是法界的同義詞，而且他通過參與法界已然轉化了它。現在他準備參與到佛法中，分有諸佛的屬性。
在論述佛法的第二大段落（II）的開始，交代菩薩已經掌握“菩提”心，獲“菩提心寶”。他已經次第掌握法界的一切法。如今他被稱作菩薩摩訶薩，他的能力與佛無異。如是，他參透了佛法，能夠“壞諸闇冥”，“令眾生得大利益”。他修行菩薩六波羅蜜中的第五個，此觀想即禪波羅蜜（dhyānapāramitā），以及第六個，般若波羅蜜（prajñāpāramitā），此波羅蜜為諸佛所有。
這種觀想使菩薩能看到諸法的真實本性，即無相性。他由此觀想看到諸法的空性，並了知［佛］法的本性，認識到它即同於真性和實性。菩薩未採取任何行動，他既未在身體上有所作為，思想上甚至也一念不生。因此，菩薩終極的“修行”可稱為“非住住”。
經文段落的結語是對菩薩修行的最終概括，並聲明無量眾生和天神因聽此法而受益。
審視經文的兩大段落，可能會發覺第二段落有些矛盾，佛陀和未明言已得佛法的菩薩在對其聽眾說法，而在第一段落中，經文明確解釋道“一切諸法不可宣說”。進而經文明白地提出，觀想是理解和轉化法界的恰當方法。不過在涉及佛法精髓時，佛陀再次以言語對他的追隨者作出解釋。這麼做是佛出於對未覺悟者的同情，而他的教導總是被理解為利益眾生的方便（upāya）。

div.head: 鐵山2號
div.p: 在山東佛教刻經題記中，《石頌》獨一無二，是一篇美文，堪稱六朝文學的傑作。佚名作者選用措辭古雅的詞彙，輔以動人的想象和生動的譬喻，扣人心弦。

《石頌》引言採用駢體，其文雖長，金石學家稱其為“序”，凡十行，分五部分。次為“頌”，篇幅短小，計兩行，以韻文寫就。序的第一部分以四言、五言或六言駢文開篇。第二部分論供養人。核心的第三部分論啟用典禮，均以散文開始；另一個部分散文見第二部分，還有兩段在第三部分。第四部分論佛經上石。第五部分論劫末，以駢體寫成，每行四字或六字。頌文本身在一段短小的引子之後，也由詩句構成，分為四個韻組，各為四句、八句、六句和四句。
第一部分如同常見的還願文，開篇宏遠，論述了佛教的教義和佛經的價值，令人感受到世界本性的無常，包括人類存在的脆弱。惟有了解護祐眾人的正確經文方能獲得解救。
第二部分介紹了刻鑿於《石頌》旁的佛經的供養人，而《石頌》則為此經的題記。匡喆及其四個弟弟的名字也列於其上，讀者由此得知他們是漢丞相匡衡的後裔。這些佛弟子品行端正，目光遠大，意識到世界處於危殆之中，“知宏綱尚缺，察地紐方傾；嘆滄海猶遷，嗟太山言落”。第二組供養人稱作“門柚杞梓，家握芳蘭”。最後提一個佛教義邑，可能貢獻了大部分錢財。
頌文的核心部分，即第三部分的詞句保存了啟用典禮的基本內容，此儀式在刻經完成之際舉行。首先，佛經的時空被加以限定。它的啟用是“在皇周大像元年，歳大淵獻，八月庚申朔，十七日丙子”，對應於公元579年9月23日。
而鐵山刻經，當然也包括《石頌》，置於一個文化的地理空間中。佚名作者先是提到鄰近的瑕丘，這是兗州的行政中心。迄今所知，瑕丘當時有一座重要的寺院和一所尼庵。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第2頁。刻經的僧侶可能與這些寺廟有關聯。
如同許多嚴肅文本的作者一樣，這位作者也將他的對象錨定於兩根軸線交叉的節點，一為主要的南北軸線，一為次要的東西軸線。在此例中，次要軸線向東延伸至尖山（即“昌巖”），向西延伸至驛道，而主要軸線向南指向有著“拂漢之峯”的嶧山（即“邾嶧”），向北指向“岱巘魏魏……排雲之嶽”。
由鐵山南坡閱讀佛經和《石頌》能夠看見南邊和東邊的山，或許還有西邊的驛道。北邊的泰山則在視線之外。只有身居佛經上方118米高的山脊，甚至更高處，譬如其後方235米高的崗山山峰，才可能望見泰山。天氣格外晴朗時，或許能夠瞥見這座94公里之外的中國聖山（），其海拔1545米，是山東省境內最高峰。

佚名作者將讀者的注意力引向嶧山和泰山的深層次原因，是由於這兩座山在政治文化史上的重要性。先有秦始皇（公元前221－210年在位）在此立碑，紀頌天下一統。公元6世紀下半葉，又有北方佛教徒於此慶賀佛光普照之下，國土獲得了新的統一。《石頌》完成於579年，577年或578年間滅佛運動肆虐於山東境內，短暫但猛烈，其時已告結束。在此之前出現了兩處刻經節文，一處於564年和570年至572年間置入嶧山，一處於575年刻於尖山。東南方的葛山將在隨後的580年出現刻經，而泰山此期則以壯觀的《金剛經》刻銘而享有盛譽，只是準確紀年已不可考。上述諸山銘刻將這個地區轉化為佛土。《石頌》作者藉由嶧山和泰山，表明這兩座莊嚴的山峰及其由來已久的帝王之氣已完全融入佛國。
啟用典禮儀式的主祭是大沙門安法師，他是一位見地頗深的僧侶，也是一位品第極高的書法家。他書丹《大集經》“穿菩提品”。題辭的最後一句提到刻經的主要原因：“遺斯勝句，以□拔世。”《石頌》的作者在此重申在第一和第二部分已提出的話題，即在“地紐方傾”的時代刊刻佛經將起“救護”之功。
第四部分講述了刻經如何製作。佚名作者描述其形制為巨碑狀：“六龍上繞，口瑩五彩□雲；雙龜下蟠，甲負三階之路。”他確信這些成就超越了古代中國佛教傳入之前的業績以及佛陀的親身說法，“于時十二那由他衆生發菩提心，一万六千天子得无生法忍。”佛所說經文只可一次得聞，刻於山石則將傳之永久。
第五部分由四個半詩節組成。作者在此重提悲嘆世界岌岌可危的第一部分，而現在將讀者的眼光引向劫末。他言之鑿鑿地表示，刻鑿於堅石之上的佛經將不會受到劫火炎風的影響。採納了當時盛行的主張，即刻於石上的佛經將歷經末法而倖存，並傳諸下一個世代，使佛陀的教義重新為人所知。
最後，頌文以簡潔的詩化語言重複序文要義，並且誇飾書法家安法師，評價其書法超越萬世書聖王羲之（303－361）。
該文除了屬於佛教頌文，還是題跋這一文體的早期範例。作者已經開始涉及後來紙本和絹本書法題跋中的內容。他的正文部分點明用意，解釋製作背景，列舉參與者，深究其意圖並讚美其品行，確認書法家之名，明確其在書法傳統中的地位，並描述和評價其美學成就。有關《石頌》作為題跋這一體裁的早期範例，見雷德侯。因而《石頌》是中國最早成熟的題跋之一，而其17米之高，或許是無法超過的體量。

一些權威人士對《石頌》的撰者和書家作出了推測。早期的金石學家孫星衍（1753－1818）斷言，《石頌》由匡喆撰寫。《山左碑目》第14844頁。不過考慮到其本人及其先祖在文中備受褒揚，不免令人訝異。吳式芬則認為那位“□璋”為作者，但未作解釋。《金石彙目分編》第21153頁。柯昌泗從此說，未作任何解釋。《語石異同評》第296頁。當代學者大多認為於作者無考，但張廣存於2006年指出，《石頌》沒有提及在鐵山3號題記中或為經主的孫洽，因而認為《石頌》可能由孫氏撰寫。 張廣存第221－224頁；第227頁。也參見下文鐵山3號的“討論”部分。

更具有挑戰性的是《石頌》書家為誰的問題。學者歷來都認為此頌書法出眾。孫葆田在1911年的著作中稱之為鄒縣摩崖石刻中最美者。他建議以此作為書法研習者的範本，同時譴責那些製作拓片的人，因為他們在字上踩踏而使之損壞。《山東金石志》第9254頁。吳式芬對此再次斷言：書寫者是安法師本人。這更難以信服，其書法在《石頌》中被吹捧到難以置信的地步。《金石彙目分編》第21153頁。柯昌泗再一次重複此言。不過，僧安道壹顯然有意留名。在相鄰的鐵山3號題記中，他被稱為“東嶺僧安道壹”，刊於醒目的中間行，置於雙線格子內。這個名字也見於洪頂山其他幾處銘刻，無疑顯出僧安道壹欲以揚名的心態。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷洪頂山3號、4號、7號、11號、15號以及16.1號。因此，不能完全排除是這位僧人自撰《石頌》並親手書丹。
在風格上，《石頌》的書法與佛經部分幾無區別。倘若《石頌》出自另一位書家，他顯然追隨安大師的風格。因此，當代研究者基本上認為這位書家是安大師的弟子之一。只有張廣存認為書寫者與作者為同一人：孫洽。儘管《石頌》的撰者和書寫者不明，但未影響其作為佛教史、書法史和文學史的一座里程碑。

div.head: 鐵山3號
div.p: 這則刻銘提到刻經項目的三位捐助人。自右開始，首先是孫洽的題記和他的官銜，稱他為“經主”。其次是僧安道壹的名字，確認他是刻經的書丹者。最後三行是閭長嵩，上有他的官銜，其中之一是“大都維那”。
張廣存深入探討《石頌》與其他題記的關係。 張廣存第221－224頁。據考，孫洽很有可能與《魏書·孫惠蔚》提及的傳主孫惠蔚（452－518）之子孫伯禮是同一人。 《魏書》第84卷；第5冊：第1852－1853頁。 孫伯禮簡短的行狀附記於他父親的傳記中，語焉不詳。不過，其中提到孫伯禮“善隸書”，“拜……寧朔將軍”，而他的父親“侍講禁內，夜論佛經”，皇帝也知道他虔信佛教。

div.head: 鐵山4號
div.p: 李巨敖的題記原本有四行，每行五至六字不等，惜不久前被毀，但可以根據拓本進行復原，這些拓本各自拓取了某行或某字。
題記載錄李巨敖的官銜，和閭長嵩（鐵山3號）一樣，他也位居“大都維那”。李巨敖和閭長嵩都曾在北齊和北周為官。李巨敖還曾任“周平陽縣功曹”。值得注意的是，滅佛運動始於北周的574年，直到佔據北齊之後的577年，李巨敖和閭長嵩二人皆曾在兩朝任官。段松苓、孫星衍和法偉堂均錄文作“南平陽縣”，很可能是早期的拓本中“周”字看上去很像“南”，後來張廣存的論文對此字作出正確的釋讀。 張廣存第224－226頁。

div.head: 刊刻技法
div.p: 從筆道橫斷面分析鐵山石經的刻鑿方法，可分為四類：

list.item: “U”形刻法。即沿筆畫中心單鏨刻成。方便快捷，適於0.4至1.7釐米寬的筆畫。鐵山刻經多採用此法。
“V”形刻法。即從筆畫兩邊線斜入鏨，雙邊線相交，自然形成。亦方便快捷，適於0.7至1釐米寬的筆畫鐫刻。鐵山《石頌》採用此法（）。
“雙鈎”刻法。即將筆畫雙邊線單鏨刻出，留出筆畫內痕。適於寬4釐米以上的筆畫。鐵山刻經部分字採用此法。
“凵”形刻法。即從筆畫兩邊線垂直入鏨，適於2.5釐米以上寬的筆畫鐫刻。鐵山刻經大字的寬筆畫採用此法。

div.head: 書體
div.p: 《石頌》撰者盛讚刻經的書法：
上述四位書法家較早的是東漢晚期的張伯英（張芝，卒於190年前後）和鍾繇（151－230），通常視為王羲之（303－361）的先驅，他們的作品在579年以前就已如迷霧。其後書法家韋誕（179－253）擅長榜書，但作品無一傳世。
王羲之的形象最為清晰。最遲在6世紀末，他已被譽為書法大家之一，這一評價延續至今。他不僅長於用筆，新意紛呈，而且楷、行、草均能。
楷書最宜於書寫宗教經文和儒家經典。凡此類作品王羲之通常採用“小楷”，每字僅一二釐米高，結體精美，節奏均勻，字裡行間宛如珠玉瓔珞。其書道家經典《黃庭經》可謂此類書體的經典傑作。
文獻可考，佛教徒癡迷於王羲之的書法。由現存的佛經可見，6世紀的寫經體接近王羲之的小楷。有關這一發展，見雷德侯。德泉さち。 所示紙本經卷即以小楷寫成，作於576年或577年，與刊刻《石頌》的年代相仿。

但是，僧安道壹及其追隨者並未採用這一書體。可以在筆記本電腦上輕易放大字體的現代讀者可能會奇怪，為何這些書經僧人不將王羲之的小楷放大到適合山東摩崖岩石表面的尺寸。不過，這是不可能的。當放大字形時，書法家不得不徹底改變審美追求和書寫姿勢。王羲之和道教、佛教的寫經人用小楷抄寫時，將手腕置於水平支撐物並只需移動手指。對於高四五釐米的字，他們活動腕關節；而書寫更大的字，則用肘部和肩膀。僧安道壹揮寫大字時有的寬逾三米，不得不在紙面或岩石上騰挪全身。他有意挑戰並踐行一種與王羲之全然對立的書法。《石頌》撰者豪邁地宣稱這種書法“精跨”先賢。
其實，僧安道壹無從效法王羲之及其書風的追隨者所形成的這種優雅流暢的書體，這種書體適於光潔的紙張和平滑的縑帛，顯然不宜於粗糙的岩石表面。
僧安道壹另闢蹊徑，再次作了不同尋常的選擇。他轉向出現於王羲之以前的古老的隸書。這種早期書體的範例是被稱作“石經”的儒家經典，公元175年至183年刻在石版之上。據說書丹者為學者蔡邕（132－192）。蔡氏書寫嚴整而規則。所示即為一種大尺幅的隸書殘片，顯示了字的結體，這是典型的隸書，比例方整，筆畫由右頓筆收起。鐵山的書法便採用這一書體。

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Tie 鐵山, at the northern edge of the city of Zoucheng 鄒城, extends 0.9 km in an east-west direction and 0.4 km in a north-south direction. Its highest elevation is at an altitude of 148 m; its foot is 110 m above sea level. Neighboring Mount Gang 崗山 lies 1.5 km to the northeast of Mount Tie. Geographically, Mount Tie is situated 116,961° east and 35,415° north, WGS84. This equals 496470 E (easting) and 3919020 N (northing), UTM zone 50S.

Today, the southern side of Mount Tie has been converted into a public park (Tieshan gongyuan 鐵山公園) with several modern buildings, including pavilions and a guard house. Rows of apartment blocks encroach from the south to about 200 m from the mountain (map, fig. and fig. ).

Close to the eastern end of the mountain is its largest rocky slope. This even and bare, triangular surface, facing south, is ca. 57 m along the base and rises up ca. 92 m, at an almost constant incline of 45–50%. The top of this rock face, covered in shrubby vegetation, is a mere 30 m below the peak of Mount Tie.

The principal slope, of which the lower western side is now also covered by vegetation, bulges slightly along its center line. It lent itself to the carving of a large text; the inscriptions have been placed here (fig. ). The sutra looks toward Mount Yang 陽山, which is 4 km away in a southeastern direction, and still further, more than 11 km away, confronts the imperial peak, Mount Yi 嶧山.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p: The design is laid out as a giant stele, with an overall height of 51.70 meters, lying obliquely on the slope, with features appropriated from free-standing stelae of the time. The lower right corner is supported by a large tortoise incised in the rock. Traces of the hexagonal pattern of its shell are still clearly discernible (fig. ). Since the animal takes up only half of the width of the base, it is assumed that two tortoises were originally carved below this enormous virtual stele.

A pair of tortoises is rarely seen beneath stelae. Figure shows a ninth or tenth century specimen from the Silla dynasty in Korea. A colophon, the Stone Hymn (Shisong
石頌, Tie 2), also mentions a pair of tortoises. No traces remain of the tortoise to the left. The posture of the recognizable animal is remarkable. It is shown frontally with the head fully stretched out and turned upward, so that eyes, nose and beak can be clearly distinguished (fig. ). The single tortoise supporting the carved “Mahayana Sutra” Stele on the northern slope of Mount Hongding (125–130) is comparable, but less well preserved.

In a rectangular field of 34.7 x 13.8 meters on the stele’s body a passage from the Vast and Universal Great Collection Sutra (Da fangdeng daji jing 大方等大集經) is engraved in two large blocks of eight columns each. Between the eighth and the ninth column, a space about one meter wide—wider than one column—is left empty. This aisle may have allowed practitioners to walk up and down, while reading or reciting the sutra texts in the adjacent columns without treading on the characters. In a photograph taken in the 1940s, when the vegetation was much less dense, the aisle seems to point in the direction of a double-pronged rock emerging from the ridge of the hill. 15. The columns each contain between 57 and 62 characters. A short, last seventeenth column comprises only six characters. Unlike the other columns, it does not begin at the top; its first character is on the same level as the character ming 名 (16/9). Further down in this column follow two isolated, smaller characters, an 安 (to the left of he 訶, 16/18) and shang 上. They are not part of the sutra text. The reconstruction of the text has demonstrated that originally 946 characters were carved. In today’s printed Taishō edition, this passage comprises only 930 characters. On the rock, two characters were omitted and eight were added. Moreover, at the beginning of some columns, between one and four characters from the end of the previous column were repeated, amounting to ten additional characters.

figure.head: 刻經下方的贔屭線刻；高繼習繪製
Outline of the tortoise carved below the sutra text; tracing by Gao Jixi

div.p: Like its three-dimensional prototype, the stele rising up the slope of Mount Tie also has a head, measuring ca. 13.2 x 18 meters (fig. ). The aisle in the stele’s body leads right up to a vertical rectangular field at the center of the head, which displays the title: Great Collection Sutra (Daji jing 大雧經). The field measuring 8.51 x 3.31 meters is encased by a frame of double lines about 30 centimeters apart; at several places it is cut through by the lateral tips of calligraphic strokes. Double horizontal lines also separate the three characters. The elaborate character ji 雧 sits on a slightly bulging area in the irregular stone surface, indicating that the text was placed on the rock with clear intent. The title is enclosed by traces of undulating lines that belong to the intertwined bodies of dragons. Comparing these lines with the décor of free-standing stelae, it appears that the dragon heads were turned outward, their snouts almost touching the upper edge of the stele body. Their crouched backs may have reached as much as 4 meters above the upper frame of the title.

figure.head: 崇福寺雙贔屭碑座，安康邑，慶州市，韓國國立慶州博物館；2010年拍攝
Stele base with a pair of tortoises from Sungboksa temple, Oedong-eup, Gyeongju. Gyeongju National Museum; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: To help preserve the inscriptions, in the 1980s, a fence was erected on top of a masonry wall, and stairs were cut inside the fence. A narrow path was also put across the stele’s head. It runs through the lower part of the character ji in the title, and the dragons to the left and right. The modern fence ignores the original shape of the sutra and is not aligned with its columns. Its triangular peak does not follow the rounded shape typical of such stele heads (fig. ). Nonetheless, visitors standing at the foot of the slope get a full impression of the monumentality of the carving (fig. ).

To the lower left of the sutra, outside of the original stele surface, but inside the modern wall, a large text entitled Stone Hymn (Shisong 石頌; Tie 2) was cut in twelve long vertical columns, forming a rectangular field of ca. 17 x 4 meters. The upper right edge of the rectangle touches the character fa
法 (16/34) of the sutra text. The field of script slopes away toward the left, so that its lower right corner is ca. 85 centimeters away from the neighboring sutra text (fig. ). Its characters are smaller than those of the sutra, but the two title characters, which are written in a stately seal script and placed above the text, measure 85 x 76 centimeters (shi) and 72 x 77 centimeters (song).

On the flat rock surface, ca. 16 meters beneath the last horizontal row of the sutra text and below its lower left corner, but still within the perimeter of the modern fence, a colophon in six columns lists three individuals by name and with their titles, Lü Changsong 閭長嵩, Seng’an Daoyi 僧安道壹 (fl. 562–579), and Sun Qia 孫洽 (Tie 3; figs. and ). The center column with the name of the monk is distinguished by an empty column on either side, and its characters are set within a grid of double lines like those of the sutra title above. These are the only two instances of such a grid on Mount Tie.

One more colophon, naming a certain Li Ju’ao 李巨敖, was carved at Mount Tie. Its location is no longer known, but rubbings in the Zoucheng Museum allow a reconstruction of the complete text (Tie 4; fig. ). The inscription was situated outside the modern wall to the east, but it was destroyed in 1960, when the area was used as a quarry. 250; 123.

div.head: Place Name
div.p: The mountain is a flat and low hill (gang 崗). It was first called Southern Mount Gang 南崗山 or Anterior Mount Gang 前崗山. The Stone Hymn refers to this name: “To the southeast of Xiaqiu 瑕丘, on the southern slope of the Southern Gang of the Great Mount Gang 大崗山南崗….”See below, Tie 2, p. . The Zou County Gazetteer (Zou Xian zhi 鄒縣志), revised by Lou Yijun 婁一均 in the fifty-fourth year of the Kangxi era 康熙 (1715), states:
div.quote: The Southern and Northern [Mount] Gang 南北兩崗 are located north of the county seat, two miles away from the town. These are the county seat’s mountains. Southern Mount Gang has an even rocky surface. 301. According to 312 and 224–225, Mount Gang 崗山 and Mount Tie 鐵山 together were called the Great Mount Gang 大崗山 during the Northern Zhou 北周 dynasty (557–581); Mount Tie was the Southern Gang and Mount Gang the Northern Gang. Even the Comprehensive Gazetteers of Shandong Province, compiled as late as the Jiajing era 嘉靖 (1522–1566) of the Ming 明 dynasty, still calls Mount Tie and Mount Gang by their old names: “岡山在鄒縣北五里，南北並起。南崗有石，平闊數丈。北岡石卓立如碑，皆有大字形跡，然剝落不可曉矣。” juan 5; 51: 349.
div.p:
The Geographical Gazetteer of Zou County in Shandong (Shandong Zou Xian dili zhi
山東鄒縣地理志 ), revised by Zhang Piju 張丕矩 in the Republican period (1912–1949), reads: “The Great Collection Sutra is carved on Anterior Mount Gang.”
25.

The Sequel to the Gazetteer of Zou County (Zou Xian xuzhi 鄒縣續志 ), revised by Wu Ruohao 吳若灝 in the Guangxu era 光緖 (1875–1908), calls this mountain Mount Tie and says that it is located “four miles north of the town, in front of Mount Gang.” 521. Local residents claim that a hole in a stone located west of the rock surface carved with the Great Collection Sutra is actually the footprint of [the Immortal] Iron-crutch Li (Li Tieguai 李鐵拐). Legend has it that he passed by carrying a sutra and, since it had been raining, it seemed best to dry the sutra in the sun on the rocky ground. The characters are the traces that the drying sutra left on the stone.A similar story is told for the Diamond Sutra carving in Sutra Stone Valley (Jingshiyu 經石峪) at Mount Tai. Ming literati inscriptions in the neighborhood of the sutra carving call this place Rock for Sunning the Sutra (pujingshi 暴經石 or shaijingshi 曬經石), probably alluding to an incident reported in the famous novel Journey to the West (Xiyou ji 西遊記) by Wu Cheng’en吳承恩 (ca. 1500–ca. 1582). On their way back from India to China, monk Xuanzang 玄奘, also known as “Tang Tripitaka 唐三藏”, who was modeled after the historical Tang dynasty monk of the same name, and his companions passed the “Attaining Heaven River” (Tongtianhe 通天河), and got wet. Thereupon, “they moved to a high cliff and opened the bundles to have them dry in the sun. To this day, a place named the Rock for Sunning the Sutra is still recognized. 卻移經於高崖上，開包曬晾。至今彼處曬經之石尚存。” See 880. For that reason, this mountain was called Mount Li Tieguai, abbreviated as Mount Tie.

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: The Zou County Gazetteer says that the Faxing Monastery 法興寺 stood at the Northern Gate of the county town. The site is today within Zoucheng City, covering circa 2,000 square meters. When, during the late Northern dynasties, the county seat was moved from its site below Mount Yi to the north, the Faxing Monastery was designated the County Monastery. When restoration were undertaken in the Yuan 元 dynasty (1271–1368), it was re-named Chongxing Monastery 重興寺. A Song dynasty brick pagoda survives on the site.

figure.head: 由刻經處眺望鄒城市景；2007年拍攝
View from the inscription site on Mount Tie toward the city of Zoucheng; photograph taken in 2007

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: The individuals involved in the carving project on Mount Tie are named in the Stone Hymn and two other colophons.

When discussing the carvings at Mount Tie, scholars usually pay great attention to the Stone Hymn . Not only does it eulogize the outstanding accomplishments of the Great Collection Sutra ’s calligraphy, but the calligraphy of the Stone Hymn itself is also remarkable. The eulogy, moreover, tells the whole story of the carving and praises the genealogy of the descendants of the Han dynasty Great Minister, Kuang Heng 匡衡. It is accepted that the Kuang family led a team to engrave the rock. In the epigraphic literature, such as the
Epigraphic Records of Shandong by Ruan Yuan 阮元, the Stone Hymn is called the “Hymn on Kuang Zhe Carving the Sutra.” This designation is still in use.

The relation between the sutra text, the Stone Hymn and the colophons is complex and reflects the interaction of government officials, a powerful clan, and the monk calligrapher. In fact, the same kind of cooperation is also apparent at Mount Jian.

In every stone carving project, the sutra donor is the most important person; he is responsible for organizing the funding and determining its use. At Mount Tie and Mount Jian, the Great Renunciant Seng’an Daoyi undertook the carving projects in collaboration with powerful local clans, namely the Kuang family at Mount Tie, and the Wei 韋 family at Mount Jian. Yet the situations of the donors of these two carving projects were quite different.

Although the inscriptions at Mount Tie were carved only four years after those at Mount Jian, these years witnessed the persecution of Buddhism and a change of dynasty. From 573, Emperor Wu of the Northern Zhou 北周武帝 (r. 561–578) persecuted the Buddhists and, after the defeat of the Northern Qi dynasty in 577, he extended this persecution into present-day Shandong. When Emperor Wu died in 578, the persecution came to an end. As three inscriptions at Mount Jian (JS 2, 4, and 7) are dated to the year 575, it is clear that this carving project was executed before the persecution reached Shandong. By contrast, the carvings at Mount Tie, from the first year of the daxiang 大像 era of the great Zhou dynasty (579), were made after the persecution had come to an end. They are much more ambitious than those at Mount Jian, and were seemingly undertaken in a period of triumph after the persecution, anticipating the renewal of Buddhism under Emperor Wen of Sui 隋文帝, who seized power in 581.

Some people have suggested that the sentence in the Hymn reading “led the people of this lay society” refers to Kuang Zhe 匡喆 and others leading the villagers in making the carving. Yet, the Kuang family members were actually neither sutra donors nor officials. The text also says that they acted “along with the like-minded persons,” and that, “to dedicate their wealth to Dharma cloisters ... they have cut into their family resources.” “Like-minded persons” refers to the community associations (yiyi 邑義 or yiyi 義邑) that were popular during the Northern dynasties for the pursuit of Buddhist merit. These organizations were formed for carving sutras or sculpting images. The stone carving was actually commissioned by the Kuang brothers and “Li Tao 李桃, Tang 湯 […], [Tang] Tuo 𡛏 , and others”. These people were all donors, who put up the capital for the project. Hence, we know that the Kuang brothers led people from a local community to carve the sutras. The community was a Buddhist association, in which the members raised money for merit-accumulation and for veneration of the Buddha. The Kuang brothers appear to have considered themselves as their leaders; however, they were not formally identified as organizers within the association, and were also not named explicitely as sutra donors. The colophons Tie 3 and Tie 4 to the sutra offer more information.

In these colophons we find: the signatures of the Great Renunciant Seng’an Daoyi and the sutra donors—the local official Sun Qia 孫洽, Magistrate of Rencheng Commandery, concurrently General who Calms the North and Great Commander-in-Chief; as well as two Great Chief Overseers, attending to the “left and right”—namely the Qi dynasty Officer on Duty of Rencheng Commandery, later the Zhou dynasty Officer on Duty of Pingyang County, Li Ju’ao 李巨敖 of Zhao Commandery; and the Qi dynasty Recruiter of Good Men and General who Pacifies Yue, later the Zhou dynasty Recorder of Rencheng Commandery, Lü Changsong 閭長嵩.

Chief Overseer is not an official title, but derives from the use of the titles for monk officials in a community association. While Li Ju’ao and Lü Changsong both held such ranks, they were not monks, nor monk officials. In this case the term Chief Overseer refers to an agent inside a community association. In votive inscriptions on sculptures and stelae donated by local associations in the Northern dynasties, the titles of Overseer and Chief Overseer often appear. They simply refer to people in charge of Buddhist merit-making activities. The term Overseer in the innumerable votive inscriptions simply means that this person has participated in making images. Consequently, the two Great Chief Overseers at Mount Tie were the managers, the executives of this carving project, and not monk officials.

Thus, among the officials, clan members and monks involved in the sutra carvings at Mount Tie, the Kuang family did not have the status of local officials, but were mainly fund-raisers, leading the devout. In other words, the Kuang brothers, Li Tao and other association members put up the capital. Local officials were acting as sutra donors; the General who Calms the North, Sun Qia, and his subordinates, Li Ju’ao and Lü Changsong, were in charge of the organization and carried out the duties of Chief Overseer. The artisans who did the carving were neither mentioned in the Stone Hymn nor in the colophons. But we should not forget that Seng’an Daoyi’s calligraphy of the Great Collection Sutra was only realized by their sweat and toil.

Finally, it should be noted that, in the Stone Hymn (Tie 2), Seng’an Daoyi is referred to just as Great Renunciant (śramaṇa), Dharma Master An 大沙門安法師. This suggest, that An 安 was probably his family name. In his comment, Li Zuoxian 李佐賢 (1807–1876) asserts: “We can be sure that the so-called Dharma Master An is An Daoyi 所謂安法師者，即安道一無疑也”. 14195. The early epigrapher, Duan Song­ling 段松苓 (1745–1800) concurs: “With regard to ‘An Daoyi’, ‘An’ is probably the family name, and ‘Daoyi’ the personal name. 安道一者，似安姓，道一名也.” Likewise, when commenting on the inscription at Mount Jian (JS 1), Duan Songling simply refers to the monk as An Daoyi 安道一, although the inscription itself reads Great Renunciant Seng’an Daoyi 沙門僧安道壹. 14844. The early epigraphers, however, did not yet know of all the Shandong inscriptions that we know today. In fact, the name An Daoyi occurs in none of them, whereas the name Seng’an Daoyi is seen most often. In the colophon, Tie 3, the monk is called Seng’an Daoyi of the Eastern Range 東嶺僧安道壹. 315, note 1, has recently argued most forcefully in favor of the name form Seng’an Daoyi. See also the discussion in 81, 116, 143. Nevertheless, it should be noted that An is a Sogdian family name, and that many bearers of this name distinguished themselves as Buddhist monks and translators. See , 77–79.

div.head: Dating
div.p: The polished cliff carvings at Mount Tie are in three parts—the sutra text, the Stone Hymn , and the colo­phons—but the relation between them is far from clear. 25–27 discuss the problem of dating. The sutra was, obviously, of prime importance, covering an area of more than one thousand square meters. The Stone Hymn occupies about one quarter of that area, and the colophons are even smaller, about one quarter of the size of the Stone Hymn .

With regard to the question of whether the carving of the Stone Hymn and the colophons followed each other, alternated with each other, or were done simultaneously, some progress was made in recent years. Aerial photographs of the polished cliff inscriptions at Mount Tie were taken as part of the Chinese-German cooperation project in 2004 (fig. ). The bird’s-eye view shows that a column was left blank at the center of Mount Tie’s sutra inscription, dividing the text into two equal parts (fig. ). The Stone Hymn is on its western side. Given the allocation of space to the two texts, it is likely that they were planned together, before the carving started. This is a different and new understanding of the words “seeking the master’s treasured brushwork.” Some scholars have earlier interpreted this phrase as implying that the search for a calligrapher came after the engraving of the sutra, and that therefore the eulogy was later than the sutra.

The Stone Hymn has an absolute date: “Seventeenth day, a bingzi day, of the eighth month, which began on a gengshen day, Jupiter in Pisces, first year of the daxiang era of the August Zhou dynasty (September 23, 579).” This form of date is rarely seen at the end of colophons. The Stone Hymn explains that Mount Tie’s sutra text was written by Seng’an Daoyi in 930 characters taken from the “Piercing the Bodhi Chapter” of the Great Collection Sutra and financed by local worthies of the Kuang clan and others, who “painted the rock, picturing a stele,” and carved the characters and the dragons above and the tortoises below. The eulogy’s verses, as well as the prose text, praise again and again the merits of the Kuang brothers, Li Tao, and others who donated their property. The eulogy also draws attention to Seng’an Daoyi’s excellent calligraphy. The wording is superlative and calls the calligraphy very striking. The writer of the Stone Hymn implies that there was no time gap between his composition and the carving of the sutra. While there was only one calligrapher, there were several donors, and certainly more than one carver; therefore, it is likely that all the work was completed in that one year, 579.

div.head: Research History
div.p: From the late 18th century onward, the inscriptions on Mount Tie became a focus of epigraphic studies. Scholars admired the big characters of the sutra inscription and the vitality of the Stone Hymn . Their records range from simple catalog entries to complete text transcriptions and critical commentaries. Thus, Mount Tie can serve as a case study for the development of epigraphy in China, as set out in the introduction to this volume.

The early epigraphers focused on individual characters; most of the time, they worked from rubbings and they rarely visited the inscription sites themselves. In consequence, their studies were unsystematic and incomplete. A remarkable beginning, however, was made by Huang Yi 黃易 (Huang Xiaosong 黃小松; 1744–1802). He not only went to the site, but also made a sketch of Mount Tie (fig. ). It is one of the 24 sketches in his album of 1797, Illustrations of Enquiries into Stelae Around Mount Dai (Dailu fangbei tu
岱麓訪碑圖). 190 and 159, fig. 3.3. For a leaf showing Mount Tie in a similar album in the Princeton University Art Museum see 474, leaf 5. For Huang Yi’s albums see above, Introduction, pp. . In his colophon, Huang Yi gives a description of the site and its engraved texts and he relates how he made rubbings on the steep and slippery cliff. He praises the calligraphy, saying “the text is heroic and beautiful, vying with the carved cliff inscription around Mount Dai.” He does not recognize the sutra text, but he identifies the donor Kuang Zhe.

In the summer of 1796, Huang Yi sent rubbings of the Stone Hymn (Tie 2) to Ruan Yuan 阮元 (1764–1849), who learned about Kuang Zhe from him. Ruan Yuan used the rubbings for his Epigraphic Records of Shandong , which appeared in the following year. In scroll 10, entitled “Stones of the Northern Zhou,” are two entries on inscriptions at what is called Minor Mount Tie (Xiao Tie Shan 小鐵山). The first one, entitled “Hymn on Kuang Zhe Carving the Sutra,” refers to the
Stone Hymn , for which a partial transcription is given. The second entry, “eight items of fragmentary inscriptions of Minor Mount Tie,” lists the colophons Tie 3 and Tie 4, as well as four excerpts from the sutra passage Tie 1. 14484–14486.

The Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World (Huanyu fangbei lu 寰宇訪碑錄 ) by Sun Xingyan 孫星衍 (1753–1818), which was published in 1803, has in scroll 2 five headings relating to Mount Tie. Yet only the first three are explicitly identified as carvings on Minor Mount Tie, namely two headings referring to the Stone Hymn (Tie 2), and one heading, “eight entries with fragmentary inscriptions on polished cliffs”, which may have been copied from Ruan Yuan’s work. Two more headings list the colophons naming Li Ju’ao from Zhao Commandery (Tie 4) and Sun Qia, General who Calms the North (Tie 3). 19873.

In his Revised Edition with Notes of the ‘Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World’ (Huan­yu fangbei lu jiaokan ji 寰宇訪碑錄校勘記), Liu Shengmu 劉聲木 (1876–1959) provides three headings on Mount Tie: The first is Kuang Zhe’s Hymn on Carving a Sutra on Minor Mount Tie , dated to the eighth month, first year of the da­xiang era; then follows the heading “eight entries with fragmentary inscriptions”, which Liu Shengmu identifies as “colophons to the Buddhist sutra”; concerning the third heading, the colophon naming Sun Qia and others (Tie 3), Liu notes that it is already contained in the eight entries with fragmentary inscriptions. 20117.

Li Zuoxian 李佐賢 (1807–1876) was one of the few epigraphers who actually went to the mountain, making a visit in 1839. In his Colophons to Epigraphica from the Stone Well Studio (Shiquan shuwu jinshi tiba 石泉書屋金石題跋), he describes the site, lists the column numbers of the inscriptions and the approximate numbers of characters in each column, and identifies “Dharma Master An” in the Stone Hymn as the same person who is named in colophon Tie 3. He relates the calligraphic style of the carving to that in Sutra Stone Valley (Jingshiyu 經石峪) on Mount Tai. Moreover, he constantly compares his findings with Ruan Yuan’s account and tries to correct Ruan’s mistakes. Li Zuoxian had seen three columnsIn fact, the colophon consists of four columns. of the now lost colophon naming Li Ju’ao (Tie 4), carved to the east of the sutra text. 14195.

Wu Shifen 吳式芬 (1796–1856), in his
Categorized Edition of an Assembled Catalog of Epigraphica (Jinshi huimu fenbian 金石彙目分編), gives a complete listing of all inscriptions on Mount Tie. It includes the
Great Collection Sutra , the Stone Hymn , and colophons with the names Sun Qia, Seng’an Daoyi, Lü Changsong, and Li Ju’ao. However, Wu Shifen, in addition, records the two characters an shang 安上, which he takes as a separate entry, thus arriving at a total of seven entries of polished cliff inscriptions on Mount Tie. 21153.

Wang Jun 汪鋆 (1816–1884–), in his
Record of Epigraphica that Passed Before my Eyes in the Twelve Ink Stones Studio (Shi’eryan zhai jinshi guoyan lu 十二硯齋金石過眼錄), lists more inscriptions than Ruan Yuan and says that the rubbings he had seen had title labels in Ruan’s handwriting. He also relies on Sun Xingyan’s Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World, but he confuses inscriptions from Mounts Jian, Tie and Gang: The two colophons in the left part of Tie 3 are found under Mount Jian, and the colophon in the right part under Mount Gang. Twenty characters from the sutra text (Tie 1) are placed under Minor Mount Tie, but here we also find inscriptions from Mount Gang. 7851–7852, 7856–7857.

The Sequel to the Gazetteer of Zou County: Epigraphy (Zou Xian jinshi xuzhi
鄒縣金石續志), compiled by Qian Tai 錢枱 and issued in 1892, 136. contains a discussion, which quotes from the Draft of the Gazetteer of Zou County (Zou Xian zhi gao
鄒縣志稿), compiled by Dong Chun 董純 and Ma Xingyi 馬星翼 in 1834. In the entry on Mount Tie, entitled “Polished cliff inscriptions from the first year of the daxiang era of the Northern Zhou,” Dong and Ma introduce the sutra with numbers of columns and characters and with quotations of the beginning and the end of the text. The numbers of columns and characters, the dating, and the verses of the Stone Hymn are also given, as well as the number of ten columns for the colophons “to the east” and “to the south of the rock.” 469a.

Sun Baotian’s 孫葆田 (1840–1911) Epigraphic Records of Shandong (Shandong jinshi zhi 山東金石志) provides detailed information on the Stone Hymn and the colophons, namely the “colophon of Sun Qia, the Northern Qi dynasty General who Calms the North”; “colophon of Seng’an Daoyi from the Eastern Range, of the Northern Qi dynasty”; “colophon of the Northern Qi dynasty Recruiter of Good Men” and the “Hymn on Kuang Zhe Carving the Sutra in the Northern Zhou dynasty”. Yet, no passage from the largest inscription, the Great Collection Sutra, is recorded. 9245–9246, 9253–9254
Kang Youwei in 1890, Ye Changshi in 1901, and Yang Shoujing in 1911, all offered high but unspecific praise.See Introduction, pp. .

In his Shallow Words on Studying Calligraphy (Xueshu eryan 學書邇言), Yang Shoujing 楊守敬 (1839–1915) praises the composition and elegance of the Stone Hymn (Tie 2). He also comments on Bao Shichen’s 包世臣 (1775–1855) error in dating the stele with the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī (Wenshu bore bei 文殊般若碑) to the Western Jin dynasty 西晉 (265–316), saying that, “Bao never saw the complete carvings on Minor Mount Tie, Mount Gang, and Mount Jian, but only the stele with the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
.” 22–23. See also above, note .
In the 1940s, Michihata Ryōshū 道端良秀 observes that the characters on the mountain are well preserved and places the inscription above all other Buddhist polished cliff inscriptions in China. 13–19.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: At Mount Tie, one sutra passage (Tie 1) and three historical inscriptions (Tie 2, 3, and 4) were carved (fig. ).

div.head: Tie 1
div.p:
The text passage carved on the body of the monumental stele at Mount Tie is drawn from Dharmakṣema’s (Tan Wuchen 曇無讖, 385–433) translation of the Vast and Universal Great Collection Sutra.T#397, 13: 1–407. For the translator Dharmakṣema see the study by 80–82. See also Mount Hongding, HDS 2, 103–107. The translation is traditionally dated to 420. The received edition of this text is made up of twelve chapters (pin 品) in a total of 60 scrolls (juan
卷). The “Chapter on the Bodhisattva of Ocean-like Wisdom” (Haihui pusa pin
海慧菩薩品), in which this passage is located today, is chapter 5 of this text, which extends over four scrolls, 8, 9, 10 and 11.T#397, 13: 46b–74c. The passage carved on Mount Tie corresponds to T#397, 13: 50a16–c13. Note that Harrist’s identification of the “Chapter on the Bodhisattva of Ocean-like Wisdom” as chapter 8 of the Great Collection Sutra (2) is misleading. As noted, it should rightly be seen as chapter 5 of the text; the passage in question happens to fall within the eighth scroll of the physical text. See also Discussion, Wang Jun, p. , Aikawa, p. .

The text of the Great Collection Sutra, however, does not seem to have been entirely stable throughout its history. Sengyou 僧祐 (445–518), in his Compilation of Notes on the Issuing of the Tripitaka (Chu sanzang jiji 出三藏記集), states that the edition of the text with which he was familiar consisted of 29 scrolls, divided into twelve chapters, the fifth of which was, as in the received edition, the “Chapter on the Bodhisattva of Ocean-like Wisdom”.T#2145, 55: 63a23–28. Zhisheng 智昇 (fl. 728–740), in his Kaiyuan Era Catalogue of Śākyamuni’s Teachings (Kaiyuan shijiao lu 開元釋教錄), says of the text version in his possession that the “Chapter on the Bodhisattva of Ocean-like Wisdom” was chapter 4 of the Great Collection Sutra.T#2154, 55: 588a16–17. The received edition of Seng­you’s text, as noted above, does not seem to support this. This textual “fluidity,” as Harrist names it, 12 n. 6; 162–163, 328, no 13. is also reflected in the fact that the Mount Tie Stone Hymn states that the sutra passage carved at the site was drawn from the “Piercing the Bodhi Chapter” (Chuan puti pin 穿菩提品). As Harrist rightly points out, there is no such chapter, at least not in the received edition. However, a text on three sheets (san zhi 三紙, approximately one page of the modern Taishō edition of the canon), with the title Sutra on Piercing the Bodhi Mind (Chuan puti­xin jing 穿菩提心經), is listed in Zhi­sheng’s catalog, where it is said to have been copied from a certain “Chapter on the Ocean-like Wisdom” associated with the Great Treasure Collection Sutra ( Ratnakūṭa-sūtra , Da baoji jing 大寶積經). However, it is not now located there.T#2154, 55: 651c25. For a discussion of the doctrinal content of this text, see the chapter on the Great Collection Sutra in 65–78. For other early versions (text witnesses), see the Dunhuang manuscripts S. 6388 and S. 613v.

Two short paragraphs from this Great Collection Sutra passage were also carved at two other sites: The paragraph on the Six Perfections (T#397, 13: 50b15–18) appears in inscription HDS 2 at Mount Hongding (see 103–107); the immediately following paragraph on the Four Immeasurable States of Mind (si wuliangxin 四無量心, T#397, 13: 50b18–20) is found in cave 2 at Southern Mount Xiangtang (Nan Xiangtang Shan 南響堂山) on the entrance wall.See 239. That text is a combination of the often carved passage of 98 characters from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī (Wenshushili suo shuo bore boluomi jing jiushiba zi jiewen 《文殊師利所說般若波羅蜜經》九十八字節文, T#232, 8: 731a15–21) and the passage on the Four Immeasurable States of Mind from the Great Collection Sutra. A rubbing of this inscription is published in 8: 46.

The text chosen for carving at Mount Tie gives a comprehensive description of the unfolding of the Buddhist path according to the Mahayana bodhisattva ideal. The Buddha poses only one question: “What is named piercing the bodhi mind?” In answering it, the Buddha unfolds for the reader the path to awakening from beginning to end. It is the mind (citta; xin 心) that makes the difference between an ordinary person and a bodhisattva, who sets out to attain the Mahayana kind of enlightenment, the Unsurpassed Correct Perfect Enlightenment. The text ends with the affirmation that this teaching of the Buddha has caused innumerable beings to embark on the bodhisattva path. They will zealously practice for the benefit of all beings still caught in the cycle of birth and death in our world of suffering. Their practice will focus on understanding the nature of the universe, the world of all dharmas, and penetrating its utmost meaning. In realizing ultimate reality, they will be able to transform the world of suffering, join the ranks of the Buddhas, and eventually ferry all beings over to the other shore. This precisely may have been the hope and motivation of the donors who initiated the grand carving project of this sutra passage.See below, Tie 2, p. .

In form and content, the body of the text is structured in two main parts: The first part expounds on the nature of the Dharma Realm ( dharmadhātu , fajie
法界). 244, has: Dharma realm: “In the Mahāyāna, dharmadhātu is used primarily to mean ‘sphere of dharma,’ which denotes the infinite domain in which the activity of all dharmas takes place—i.e., the universe. It also serves as one of several terms for ultimate reality…the purpose of Buddhist practice is to recognize and partake in this realm of reality.” It consists of a well-planned sequence of common Buddhist doctrines that are “known” or “fully known” (zhi 知 or liaozhi 了知) to the bodhisattva, and of contemplations (guan
觀) that lead him to a deeper kind of knowledge and thereby to ever more advanced contemplations. The various bodhisattva practices are neatly presented in lists and mainly written in parallel prose. The text’s second main part elaborates on the essence of the Buddha-Dharma (buddha­dharma, fofa
佛法). 151, says on the buddhadharma: “In Sanskrit, ‘the teachings of the Buddha’; one of the closest Indian equivalents to what in English is called ‘Buddhism’… The term is also used with reference to the ‘unshared factors’…, a list of eighteen (or sometimes as many as 140) special qualities (dharma) that are unique to the buddhas, such as their perfect mindfulness, and their inability to make a physical or verbal mistake, or for all the qualities that together make up a buddha.” 238–239, is reluctant to translate the Pali ‘dhamma’ (or Sanskrit dharma) as ‘teaching’, and writes: “In contexts where there is a question of the Buddhist tradition it is all too easy to render ‘dhamma’ as ‘teaching’ or ‘doctrine’, yet this fails to convey a fundamental nuance of the notion of dhamma. There is in the appeal to the seven sets a kind of equivocation that is inherent in the notion of dhamma as at once truth itself—truth that is to be directly known and realized—and teachings about the truth—teachings that indicate how the truth is to be directly known and realized.” It is structured less strictly and is written as an exhortation, in which the Buddha addresses the practitioner five more times as “good man”. This form of address is suitable for lay followers of the Buddha and thus to any reader of the text at Mount Tie.
In a second version, the Parallel Text, the layout corresponds to the structure of the text (below, pp. ). The contents of the carved text may be summarized thus:

In the introduction, the Buddha makes known that he is about to expound on how the bodhi mind (putixin 菩提心), the mind of enlightenment, can be realized. The mind of an ordinary person “gives rise to the semblance of a ‘Self’,” because an ordinary person does not question his existence and identity as a particular individual. For such a person to become a follower of the Buddha, it is necessary to first “put forth the bodhi mind” (fa putixin
發菩提心) in order to become a bodhisattva. However, the Buddha issues a first warning: The bodhi mind is nothing to be attached to, nothing to crave for, nothing to yearn for, and not even something to contemplate on, because grasping, craving, yearning—and even contemplating, at least of the wrong kind—are practices that give rise to, once again, the mind of an ordinary person and not that of a bodhisattva. When all attachment, craving, yearning and contemplation ceases, a bodhisattva will be able to “make the mind quiescent and tranquil”. Only a quiescent mind ensures the right kind of contemplation that penetrates the “deep Dharma Realm” and the “Buddha-Dharma”.

These two concepts mentioned at the end of the introduction, the Dharma Realm and the Buddha-Dharma, can be considered as headings for the two main parts, I and II, of the body of the text. The first part, the exposition on the Dharma Realm, can be further divided into three sub-sections; the first on recognizing the Dharma Realm I.1, the second on partaking in the Dharma Realm I.2, and the third on transforming the Dharma Realm I.3. Through observing the nature of all dharmas, the bodhi­sat­tva needs to understand first how the world is truly constituted, before he is able to engage in and complete his manifold practices that will finally transform the world into the Dharma Realm of Ultimate Reality.

The first sub-section, I.1, on recognizing the Dharma Realm, starts with the foundation of the Buddhist worldview, the teaching of the Twelve Fold Chain of Dependent Origination ( dvādaśa-astanga pratītya­samutpāda , shier yinyuan 十二因緣). All dharmas in the universe are dependent on each other, continuously emerging and ceasing as a result of causes and conditions. Nothing stays the same; nothing abides, not even for a fraction of a second. Concepts of eternity or existence are nothing but illusion; all things have no self or core that could be identified as a support for ‘selfness’. By contemplating on dependent origination, the bodhisattva gains insight into the emptiness that lies at the core of all phenomena, and he knows that there is neither self, nor subject, nor actor.

At this point, a practitioner may be in danger of succumbing to nihilism; a bodhisattva, however, believes in the three modes of samadhi, which reveal that emptiness is the key for the correct understanding of the world as it is.

Having thus understood the conditioned nature of all dharmas, the bodhisattva next turns to the Five Aggregates (skandha, wu yun 五蘊), which make up the composite nature of all beings, and he recognizes that these are equally empty. Foam, bubbles, and a mirage are metaphors for impermanence and insubstantiality, along with the coreless trunk of the banana tree and deceiving illusions.

His next contemplation leads the bodhisattva to comprehend that the phenomena of the object world are uncreated and unmoved, and that, in an equal fashion, all beings are unborn, because they offer no support for a ‘self’, as their deceitful sense organs and never resting minds make them believe. In this sense, all dharmas of the universe, including the uncreated phenomena of the outside world, as well as all the unborn sentient beings, are not distinct from each other. They are “of one flavor, one vehicle, one way, one origin”.

The bodhisattva then contemplates on the sounds of the world, insubstantial and incoherent as they are, and he realizes that speech, which is composed of sounds, is inadequate to describe the real nature of dharmas. When the sutra here takes up ‘sounds’ or ‘voices’ as objects for meditation, it may be alluding to the renowned Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds from the Lotus Sutra, who listens and responds to the voices of the world’s suffering beings. And indeed, the text continues with a bodhisattva’s full understanding of the Four Noble Truths ( ārya-satya ; si di
四諦) on suffering, its accumulation, the possibility of its extinction, and the path to be taken.

Now, the bodhisattva “completely knows the Path”, and, in the second subsection, I.2, he is able to partake in the Dharma Realm by practicing according to a concept that was known in all Buddhist traditions as the Thirty-seven Factors of Enlightenment ( saptatriṃśad­bodhi­pakṣikā­dharmāḥ ; sanshiqi daopin 三十七道品). 14. These Thirty-seven Factors are made up of a set of seven lists of particular practices, 280 concludes that “the sequence of the seven sets was already firmly established as a distinct and separate list in the period of the four primary Nikāyas [of the Pāli canon].” namely:

list.item: The Four Stations of Mindfulness ( catvāri smṛtyupasthānāni ; si nianchu 四念處).
The Four Correct Efforts ( catvāri samyak-prahāṇāni ; si zhengqin 四正勤).
The Four Bases of Magical [Power] ( catvāra ṛddhipādāḥ ; si shenzu 四神足 or si ruyizu 四如意足).
The Five Roots [of Goodness] ( pañcêndriyāṇi ; wu gen 五根).

The Five Powers ( pañcabalāni ; wu li
五力).
The Seven Limbs of Awakening ( sapta­bodhyaṅgāni ; qi juezhi 七覺支 or qi juefen 七覺分).
The Eightfold Correct Path ( āryā­ṣṭāṅgika­mārga ; ba zhengdao 八正道).

div.p: Striving to free himself from the cognitive obstructions that prevent him from apprehending true reality, the bodhisattva investigates his own mind and feelings according to a set of practices called the Four Stations of Mindfulness ( catvāri smṛtyupasthāna ; si nianchu 四念處). Realizing the emptiness of his body, his feelings and his mind, as well as that of all phenomena of the outside world, the bodhisattva attains knowledge of the Dharma Realm.

The next two sets of practices are not listed in full in the text at Mount Tie. The Four Correct Efforts are summarized as Correct Effort (zhengqin 正勤), and the Four Bases of Magical Powers as Cultivation of the Bases of Supernatural Powers (ruyizu 如意足). The following four sets of practices are, however, listed in full: the Five Roots [of Goodness], the Five Powers, the Seven Limbs of Awakening and the Eightfold Correct Path. Each factor in these four sets is not only named, but also explained in a short expression, yielding a total of 25 lines of parallel prose. Each line is composed of eight characters, of which four explain the meaning of a factor, and four give its name. In the case of the Eightfold Correct Path, two more four-character phrases are added, one to explain that Correct Action, Correct Livelihood and Correct Vigorous Effort refer to “body, speech and mind”, and one to explain that Correct Mindfulness and Correct Concentration have an ethical dimension and touch upon problems of “the good or the not good”. By means of these short four-character explanations, the text passage from the “Piercing the Bodhi Mind Chapter” of the Great Collection Sutra interprets the long-known Thirty-seven Factors of Enlightenment in a particular Mahayana fashion. Because the Thirty-seven Factors are practiced with the mind of a bodhisattva, they become the basis for the transformation of the Dharma Realm into Ultimate Reality.

The third sub-section on the Dharma Realm, I.3, states that the nature of the Real Attributes is quiescence and tranquility. In their ultimate meaning, the Three Attributes that mark all existence, Impermanence, Suffering and No-self, are pure. In addition, what is conventionally designated as clarity and purity is indeed the Great Purity of Ultimate Reality. By contrast with the Thirty-seven Factors of Enlightenment known from other traditions, the bodhisattva now partakes in the Dharma Realm by completing particular Mahayana bodhisattva practices, namely the Six Perfections ( ṣaṭpāramitā ; liu boluomi
六波羅蜜) and the Four Immeasurable States of Mind ( catvāri apramāṇāni ; si wuliangxin 四無量心).In this respect, the text passage from the Great Collection Sutra presented here may be characterized as an expanded list of the seven sets on the Thirty-seven Factors of Enlightenment, which are found in many Mahayana sutras. 275, quotes one example from the Sanskrit version of the Perfection of Wisdom Sutra. As a result, he gains insight to the wisdom that all dharmas, without exception, are (1) in the future, pure; (2) in the past, manifold; (3) in the present, without self. The bodhisattva has thus not only mastered past, present and future, he has understood Ultimate Reality, now a synonym for the Dharma Realm, and he has already transformed the world by partaking in it. Now, he is prepared to join the Buddha-Dharma and to share the qualities of all Buddhas.

At the beginning of the second main part, II, on the Buddha-Dharma, the bodhisattva is said to have mastered the bodhi mind and to have attained “the jewel of piercing the bodhi mind”. He has subsequently accomplished mastery with respect to all dharmas of the Dharma-Realm. He is now called a bodhisattva-mahāsattva , and his abilities are in no way different from those of a Buddha. Thus, he has fully penetrated the Buddha-Dharma; he is able to “destroy the darknesses” and he “causes beings to attain great benefit”. He practices the fifth of the Six Perfections of a bodhisattva, the contemplation named dhyānapāramitā , Perfection of Meditation, together with the sixth perfection, prajñāpāramitā , the Perfection of Wisdom, which is possessed by all Buddhas.

This kind of contemplation enables the bodhi­sat­tva to see the true nature of all dharmas, which is Devoidness of Attributes. With his contemplation, he sees the emptiness of all dharmas and he knows the nature of the [Buddha-]Dharma, recognizing that it is equal to the True Nature and the Real Nature. As the bodhisattva does not take any action, he does not perform in any way and he does not even form one single thought in his mind. Therefore, the ultimate ‘practice’ of a bodhisattva can be called “Abiding that is Non-Abiding”.

With this final characterization of the practice of a bodhisattva, the sutra passage closes, but not without affirming that numerous human beings as well as gods benefited immensely from listening to it.

Looking at the two main parts of the text, it may be surprising that the Buddha, and, implicitly, the bodhisattva, who has already attained the Buddha-Dharma, is, in the second part, speaking to his listeners, while in the first part, the text has explained explicitly that “all dharmas, without exception, cannot be expressed in speech”. Furthermore, the text has clearly presented contemplation as the apropriate means to comprehend and transform the Dharma Realm. However, when it comes to the essence of the Buddha-Dharma, the Buddha, again, speaks in words to his followers. The reason for doing so is that the Buddha preaches out of compassion for all the unawakened, and his teachings are always to be understood as skillful means ( upāya ; fangbian 方便) for the benefit of all beings.

div.head: Tie 2
div.p: The Stone Hymn is the most extraordinary of all colophons accompanying engraved Buddhist sutras and sutra passages in Shandong. It is a beautiful piece of writing, a superb example of the literature of the time. The anonymous author chose an elegant and rare vocabulary and arrests the reader with evocative imagery and vivid metaphors.

The Stone Hymn consists of an introductory text mostly in parallel prose (pianti
駢體), which, in spite of its length, epigraphers have called the Preface (xu 序). It has five parts in ten columns. The short hymn proper (song
頌) follows in two columns of rhyming verse. The first part of the Preface sets the scene in parallel lines of four, five, and six characters. The second part on the donors and the central third part on the dedication, each begin with a few words in prose; another short prose section is found in the second part, and two more in the third part. The fourth part on the sutra engraving, and the fifth part on the end of the eon are entirely written in parallel prose, with lines of four or six characters. The hymn proper, after a short introduction, also consists of poetic language in four rhyme groups of eight, eight, six, and four lines.

The first part, as is usual in votive texts, opens up a wide perspective on the Buddhist teaching and the value of sutras. It evokes the ephemeral nature of the world, including our fragile human existence. Rescue can only come through the knowledge of the correct texts that save and protect.

The second part introduces the donors of the sutra engraved next to the Stone Hymn, and to which the Stone Hymn is the colophon. Kuang Zhe and his four younger brothers are listed by name, and here the reader learns that they are descendants of the Chief Minister of the Han dynasty, Kuang Heng. These virtuous and insightful disciples of the Buddha are aware of the precarious state of the cosmos, “knowing that the vast hawsers of heaven have long been deficient and discerning that the earth’s pivot is near collapse, they sighed that yet the great blue sea also changes, and bemoaned that even Mount Tai falls.” A second group of donors are called “willows and catalpas of their lineages, fragrant orchids of their families”. Finally, there is a Buddhist lay society; it may have contributed most of the funds.

The words of part three, the central part of the text, preserve the essence of the dedication ritual, which must have been performed when the engraving of the sutra was completed. First, the sutra is anchored in space and time. It was dedicated: “on the seventeenth day, a bingzi day, of the eighth month, which began on a gengshen day, Jupiter in Pisces, first year of the daxiang era of the August Zhou dynasty.” This elaborate date corresponds to September 23, 579.

Then the sutra on Mount Tie—and, implicitly, the Stone Hymn—are placed in a cultural geography. The author begins by naming Xiaqiu in the vicinity, which was the administrative center of Yanzhou 兗州 Prefecture. It is known that there were, at the time, an important monastery and a nunnery at Xiaqiu.See 20. The monks, who did the engraving, may have had connections with these institutions.

Like many writers of solemn text, the present author, too, moors his subject at a node where two axes cross, a major north-south axis, and a secondary east-west axis. In this case, the secondary axis reaches, in the east, to Mount Jian 尖山 (here called Chang Cliff 昌巖) and, in the west, to the post road, while the main axis points to Mount Yi 嶧山 (here called Mount Zhu Yi 邾嶧) in the south, which rises “majestically… its peak reaching the Milky Way,” and in the north to the “looming magnificence of Mount Dai 岱 (Mount Tai 泰山), … that sacred mountain parting the clouds.”

The mountains in the south and east, and probably the post road in the west, could be seen by someone reading the sutra and the Stone Hymn while standing on Mount Tie’s southern slope. Mount Tai in the north, however, is beyond sight. Only if the beholder climbs up to the 118 meter high ridge above the sutra and, even further, to the peak of Mount Gang behind it, at 235 meters, then no other range will obstruct his view toward Mount Tai. On an unusually clear day, he might be able to make out this most sacred of all holy mountains in China, 94 kilometers away (fig. ). At 1,545 meters, its peak is the highest in Shandong.

The deeper reason, why the writer draws the reader’s attention to the two mountains, Yi and Tai, is their prominence in political and cultural history. The First August Emperor of Qin (Qin Shi Huangdi 秦始皇帝; r. 221–210 BCE) had stelae erected there to commemorate and celebrate his unification of the realm. In the second half of the sixth century, Buddhists in northern China now rejoiced in celebrating a new unification of their land, this time under the Buddha. When the Stone Hymn was completed in 579, the short but atrocious Buddhist persecution, which had raged in Shandong in 577/578, had come to an end. In earlier years, two sutra passages had already been placed on Mount Yi in 564 and in 570–572, as well as on Mount Jian in 575. Mount Ge 葛山 to the south-east would be engraved in the following year, 580, and Mount Tai was honored with a spectacular inscription of the Diamond Sutra (Jingang jing 金剛經) in these same years; but its precise date has been lost. Together, the inscriptions on these mountains transformed the region into a land of the Buddha. By invoking Mount Yi and Mount Tai, the author of the Stone Hymn asserts that these two venerable peaks—including their ancient imperial aura—had now been fully integrated into the Buddhist realm.

The officiant of the dedication ritual was the Great Renunciant, Dharma Master An, a monk of deepest insight and a calligrapher of the highest order. He wrote out the “Piercing the Bodhi Chapter” of the Great Collection Sutra. The last sentence in the dedication names the fundamental reason for the entire enterprise: “to leave behind these surpassing words in order […] to rescue the world.” Here the author of the Stone Hymn takes up the topic that he had already raised in the first and second parts, namely that the engraved chapters from the Buddhist scriptures will “save and protect” in a time, when the “earth’s pivot is near collapse.”

In his fourth part, the author narrates how the engraved sutra was produced. He describes the format, which had the shape of an overwhelming stele: “Six dragons encircle it above, their mouths glowing with five-colored [. . .] clouds; two tortoises crouch below, their shells supporting the path of three steps.” He is sure that these achievements surpass similar ancient, pre-Buddhist feats in China, as well as the occasion when the Buddha delivered the sermon himself as “twelve nayuta of beings put forth the bodhi mind, and sixteen thousand gods attained the Acceptance that Dharmas are Without Birth.” The sutra preached by the Buddha was only heard once; engraved into the rock, however, it will last forever.

The fifth part consists of four and a half stanzas. Here, the author, while harking back to his first part, where he bemoaned the dire state of the world, now directs the reader’s view to the end of our eon. He assures us that the sutra hewn into the solid rock will not be affected by the scorching winds of the final inferno. With this, he takes up a notion, which was prevalent at his time, namely, that sutras carved into stone would survive beyond the end of the Dharma (mofa 末法). In the next world age, they will be available and make the teachings of the Buddha known again.

figure.head: 從崗山眺望泰山的虛擬視線圖，來源：谷歌地球™
Virtual sightline from Mount Gang to Mount Tai as provided by Google™Earth

div.p: Finally, the hymn proper repeats in succinct, poetic language salient elements from the introduction. The one major addition is the extravagant praise of the calligrapher, the Dharma Master An, whom the author rates more highly than the all time sage of calligraphy, Wang Xizhi 王羲之 (303–361).

In addition to being a Buddhist hymn with preface, as analyzed here, the text can also be read as an early example of the literary genre of the colophon. The author anticipates with intense precision those topics that were to be addressed in later colophons appended to works of calligraphy on paper and silk. He talks about the main text, highlighting its significance; he explains the circumstances under which it was made; he names the people involved; he explores their motives and praises their virtue; he identifies the calligrapher, defines his position in the calligraphic tradition, and he describes and evaluates his aesthetic achievement.For the Stone Hymn as an early example of a colophon genre see . The Stone Hymn thus is one of the earliest fully-fledged colophons in China and, with its height of 17 meters, perhaps the largest ever.

Several authorities have made suggestions as to who wrote the text of the Stone Hymn, and who the anonymous calligrapher may have been. The early epigrapher Sun Xingyan 孫星衍 (1753–1818) asserts that Kuang Zhe composed the Stone Hymn. 14844. This, however, would be surprising, given that he and his ancestor are highly praised in the text. Wu Shifen 吳式芬 (1796–1856) claims, without further explanation, that a certain [...] Zhang (□璋) was the author. 21153. Repeated by Ke Changsi 柯昌泗 (1899–1952), 296, again without any explanation. Most modern scholars profess ignorance about the authorship, but in 2006 Zhang Guangcun 張廣存 pointed out that the text of the Stone Hymn does not mention Sun Qia, who appears as sutra donor in the colophon Tie 3, and, therefore, he argues that the Stone Hymn was likely compiled by him. 221–224, 227. See also the following discussion of Tie 3.

Even more challenging is the question, who the calligrapher of the Stone Hymn was. All authors agree on its high quality. Sun Baotian, writing in 1911, calls it the most beautiful among the polished cliff inscriptions in Zou County. He recommends it as a model for practitioners of calligraphy, but at the same time condemns those who take rubbings, trample over the characters and damage them. 9254. Again Wu Shifen makes a bold claim: the calligrapher was the Dharma Master An himself. This is even more astonishing, because his calligraphy is eulogized in the Stone Hymn almost beyond belief! 21153. Again, Ke Changsi concurs. Yet, obviously, the Master knew how to have himself presented. In the adjacent colophon, Tie 3, where he is called “Seng’an Daoyi of the Eastern Range,” the characters in the distinguished central column are encased in a grid of double lines. The name is also known from several inscriptions on Mount Hongding 洪頂山, showing how, without hesitation, Seng’an Daoyi set himself in the scene. HDS 3, 4, 7, 11, 15, 16.1. Thus, it cannot be completely ruled out, that the monk himself composed the text of the Stone Hymn and also wrote it out in his own hand.

Stylistically, the Stone Hymn calligraphy hardly differs from that of the sutra. If the Stone Hymn was written by another calligrapher, he certainly followed Master An’s style closely. Most modern authors therefore assume that he was one of his disciples.Only Zhang Guangcun suggests that the calligrapher was the same as the author, Sun Qia. Although the identities of the author and of the calligrapher remain a mystery, the Stone Hymn is a milestone in the history of Buddhism, calligraphy and literature.

div.head: Tie 3
div.p: This one inscription names three contributors to the sutra project. It starts at the right with a colophon of Sun Qia with his official titles. He is named as “Sutra Donor.” Then follows the name of Seng’an Daoyi, identified as the monk who has written the sutra carved above. Thereafter, follows Lü Changsong in three columns, giving his official titles, among which is “Great Chief Overseer.”

Zhang Guangcun 張廣存 has explored in some detail the question of the relationship between the Stone Hymn and the colophons. 221–224. He argues that Sun Qia is in all likelihood identical with Sun Boli 孫伯禮, who is mentioned in the biography of his father, Sun Huiwei 孫惠蔚 (452–518), in the
History of the Wei Dynasty (Weishu 魏書 ).
juan 84; 5: 1852–1853. Because Sun Boli’s short biography is attached to his father’s, not much is noted about him. Nevertheless, he is said to have “excelled in clerical script (lishu 隸書)”, and he was appointed “General who Calms the North.” His father was “an Imperial Tutor in the palace, reading Buddhist sutras at night,” and was known to the Wei Emperor for his sincere faith in Buddhism.

div.head: Tie 4
div.p: The colophon by Li Ju’ao originally comprised four columns with five or six characters each. It was destroyed some time ago, but has survived in rubbings taken either from each single column or from single characters.

The colophon gives Li Ju’ao’s official titles and identifies him with the same title, “Great Chief Overseer”, as Lü Changsong (Tie 3). Li Ju’ao and Lü Changsong held official posts under both the Qi and the Zhou dynasties. Li was “Zhou dynasty Officer on Duty of Pingyang County.” Given the fierce Buddhist persecution, which began in the Northern Zhou in 574 and was extended to the Northern Qi territory after it had been conquered by the Northern Zhou in 577, it is worth noting that both men, whose Buddhist inclinations must have been known, held positions under both dynasties. Duan Songling, Sun Xingyan, and Fa Weitang all transcribed “South Pingyin County,” probably on the basis of erroneous rubbings; however, in the article by Zhang Guangcun the correct character was identified. 224–226.

figure.head: 《石頌》書法局部；2005年拍攝
Calligraphy of the Stone Hymn; photograph taken in 2005

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p:
Having examined the carving of the sutra and the colophons through a transverse section analysis of its strokes, we conclude that the techniques employed fall into four categories:
U-shaped carving: the carver chisels in one movement along the middle of the stroke. Convenient and fast, this technique is suitable for engravings of strokes 0.4–1.7 cm wide. Many characters of the sutra text are carved by this method.
V-shaped carving: the carver chisels slanting lines from both sides of a stroke, so that these lines intersect and the stroke naturally takes shape. Convenient and fast, this technique is suitable for carving of strokes 0.7–1 cm wide. The Stone Hymn is carved by this method (fig. ).
Double-outline carving: the carver chisels only the outline on either sides of a stroke, so that the interior of the stroke remains untouched. This technique is suitable for strokes that are more than 4 cm wide. Some characters of the sutra text are carved by this method.
Rectangular U-shaped carving: the carver chisels vertical lines on both sides of a stroke. This technique is suitable for strokes more than 2.5 cm wide. The broad strokes of the large characters of the sutra text are carved by this method.

div.head: Style of Calligraphy
div.p: The colophon writer of the Stone Hymn makes a remarkable statement about the calligraphy of the main text:

lg.l: Its refinement surpasses [Wang] Xi[zhi] and [Wei] Dan (fig. ),
its marvels exceed [Zhang Bo]ying and [Zhong] You (fig. );
as dragons coiling in the mist,
like phoenixes soaring in the sky.
