div.p: Of the four calligraphers, the earliest two, Zhang Boying 張伯英 (Zhang Zhi 張芝; d. ca. 190) and Zhong You 鐘繇 (151–230), lived at the end of the Han dynasty; they are often called forerunners of Wang Xizhi 王羲之 (303–361), but their oeuvres, already obscure by 579, have remained shadowy ever since. The third calligrapher, Wei Dan 韋誕 (179–253), is remembered as being proficient in writing large characters on tablets for palace halls, yet none of his works survives either.

Wang Xizhi, however, has a very clear profile. By the end of the sixth century, he was hailed as one of the greatest calligraphers who had ever lived, an assessment that has stayed with him down to today. In addition to being a superb master of the brush, with an inexhaustible versatility in creating calligraphic forms, Wang is credited with writing exemplary pieces in three types of script: regular script (kaishu 楷書), cursive running script (xingshu 行書) and fully cursive draft script (caoshu 草書).

For religious scriptures, as well as for Confucian classical texts, only a regular script was appropriate. In this domain, Wang practised the so-called small regular script (xiaokai 小楷). The characters are only about 1–2 centimetres high; they are exquisitely shaped and flow smoothly through the columns, like beads on a string or the pearls of a necklace. Wang’s most compelling work in this style is his copy of the Daoist Scripture of the Yellow Court (Huangting jing 黃庭經).

figure.head: 敦煌寫本《佛說生經》，576/577年；法國國家圖書館，東方手稿部（伯希和2965號）；來源：gallica.BnF.fr

Sutra on Former Lives Spoken by the Buddha (Foshuo sheng jing 佛說生經), from Dunhuang, Gansu Province, China, 576/577. Bibliothèque nationale de France, Manuscrits orientaux (Pelliot chinois 2965). Source: gallica.BnF.fr

div.p: Literary sources confirm that Buddhists were infatuated with Wang Xizhi’s calligraphy, and with the help of extant sutras one can trace how, during the sixth century, Buddhist scribes moved ever closer to Wang’s small regular script.For this development see ; . Figure shows a detail of a sutra scroll on paper written in this script in 576/577, in the same years in which the Stone Hymn was engraved.

However, Seng’an Daoyi and his fellow calligraphers did not adopt this style. A modern reader, who can enlarge fonts on his laptop with ease, might wonder why these monks did not enlarge Wang’s small script to a size that suited the rock surfaces of the Shandong cliffs. This, however, would have been utterly impossible. The aesthetic ambition and the body language of the calligrapher had to change completely as the scale of his characters was increased. Wang Xizhi and other Daoist and Buddhist scribes, who produced religious scriptures in small standard script, placed their wrists on a horizontal support and just moved their fingers. For characters four or five centimetres high, they moved their wrist, for still larger characters they involved their elbow and shoulder. When Seng’an Daoyi brushed his huge characters, some of which are more than 3 meters in width, he had to move his entire body over the paper or the rock. In conscious defiance, he practiced a kind of calligraphy that was utterly antagonistic to that of Wang Xizhi. The colophon writer proudly claims that “its refinement surpasses” that of the early master.

In fact, even if he had wanted to, Seng’an Daoyi could not have used the elegant fluent style, which Wang Xizhi and his followers developed for writing on clean paper and smooth silk, as it was obviously not suited for rough stone surfaces. Instead, the monk once again made an extraordinary choice. He harked back to the ancient clerical script (lishu 隸書) of the pre-Wang Xizhi period. Famous examples of that archaic script were the so-called Stone Classics (Shijing 石經), the Confucian classical texts, which had been carved on stone tablets between the years 175 and 183. The scholar, Cai Yong 蔡邕 (132–192), is said to have written the calligraphy. Cai wrote in an orderly and regular fashion—a surviving fragment shows the construction of his characters, typical for clerical script, with their distinctive blocky proportions, and the brushstrokes drawn out to the right (fig. ). The calligraphy on Mount Tie transposes this type of script at an unruly, energetic and monumental scale.

figure.head: 熹平石經殘塊，約175年，上海博物館藏
Fragment of the Stone Classics; dated circa 175; Shanghai Museum. Courtesy of Shanghai Museum
